questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of the parliamentary system of government?
options: Ministers are usually members of parliament
The Prime Minister is politically responsible to the parliament
The Head of State is the powerful organ of government
The Head of Government may advise the Head of State to dissolve parliament
questions.explanation: In a parliamentary system, the Head of State is usually ceremonial while the Head of Government (Prime Minister) exercises real power.
questions.hint: Look for the option that describes a powerful Head of State - that's more characteristic of presidential systems.

questions.question: Capitalism is an economic system in which
options: the economy of the State is centrally planned and controlled
Private persons are permitted to undertake enterprises
accumulatation of private property is forbidden
the means of production are owned and controlled by the State
questions.explanation: Capitalism is characterized by private ownership of the means of production and private individuals undertaking business enterprises for profit.
questions.hint: Think about which option describes free enterprise and private ownership.

questions.question: The process of depriving persons of the right of voting is called
questions.explanation: Disenfranchisement is the revocation of the right to vote, often due to criminal conviction or other legal restrictions.
questions.hint: The prefix 'dis-' means to remove or reverse. 'Enfranchisement' means giving voting rights.

questions.question: Bicameralsm refers to
options: the process of voting in the legislature
the upper chamber in a legislature
questions.explanation: Bicameralism is the practice of having two legislative chambers (e.g., Senate and House of Representatives).

questions.question: The principle of the separation of powers implies that the three main organs of government work
options: together in the interest of other nations
questions.explanation: Separation of powers means each branch has distinct functions but they also work cooperatively through checks and balances.
questions.hint: They are separate but must work together to govern effectively.

questions.question: The main function of the judiciary is to
options: serve as the watchdog of the Executive
execute the laws of the land
questions.explanation: The judiciary interprets laws, resolves disputes, and administers justice in accordance with the constitution.
questions.hint: Courts and judges interpret and apply the law.

questions.question: A totalitarian government is
options: a government that aspires to control every aspect of a citizen's life
a government of the people, by the people, and for the people
questions.explanation: Totalitarianism is a form of government where the state holds total authority over society and seeks to control all aspects of public and private life.
questions.hint: 'Total' means all-encompassing control over citizens' lives.

questions.question: An unwritten consititution operates in
questions.explanation: Great Britain (UK) has an unwritten constitution consisting of statutes, conventions, and common law rather than a single codified document.
questions.hint: The UK doesn't have a single written constitution document.

questions.question: A sovereign state is one
options: whose constitution can only be changed by a military government
where its citizens can speak without fear or favour
in which sovereignty is invested in the military
whose government decisions are made independent of foreign interference
questions.explanation: A sovereign state has supreme authority within its territory and independence from external control in making decisions.
questions.hint: Sovereignty means self-governing and free from outside control.

questions.question: Representative Democracy is best characterized by
options: free elections and proper register of voters
proper constituencies and a real choice of candidates
questions.explanation: Representative democracy is best characterized by proper constituencies that allow fair representation and a real choice of candidates for voters to select from.
questions.hint: It's about having genuine options and fair representation areas.

questions.question: The primary function of a legislature is
questions.explanation: The primary function of the legislature is to make laws. Other functions include oversight and representation, but lawmaking is the core function.
questions.hint: The word 'legislature' comes from 'legis' meaning law.

questions.question: According to Marxist theory, those who own and control the means of production in a capitalist society are
questions.explanation: In Marxist theory, the bourgeoisie are the capitalist class who own the means of production (factories, land, resources) and exploit the working class (proletariat).
questions.hint: The term for the wealthy capitalist class that owns production.

questions.question: While political parties aim at forming a government pressure groups aim at
questions.explanation: Pressure groups seek to influence government decisions and policies without seeking to form a government themselves.
questions.hint: They 'pressure' the government to adopt certain policies.

questions.question: When the electorate vote for representatives who in turn vote on their behalf we say it is
questions.explanation: Indirect election is a process where voters elect representatives who then vote on their behalf to choose officials (e.g., electoral college system).
questions.hint: The opposite of direct election where voters vote directly for candidates.

questions.question: An election which is conducted to fill a vacant seat in a legislature is called a
questions.explanation: A by-election (or bye-election) is held to fill a political office that has become vacant between general elections.
questions.hint: It's held 'by' itself to fill one specific vacancy.

questions.question: Which of the following countries does NOT operate a Federal constitution
questions.explanation: France operates a unitary system of government, not a federal constitution. The USA, Canada, and Nigeria all have federal systems where power is shared between central and regional governments.
questions.hint: Think about which country is known for its centralized governance structure.

questions.question: The major advantage of the secret ballot is that
options: it is faster than other systems
nobody can be prevented from voting
it ensures the anonymity of each voter
losers can ask for another secret vote
questions.explanation: The secret ballot ensures voter anonymity, protecting voters from intimidation, coercion, or retaliation for their voting choices.
questions.hint: It's called 'secret' for a reason - no one knows who you voted for.

questions.question: Which of these statements is CORRECT about Proportional Representation?
options: It makes the assembly representative of all citizens
It gives the parties seats in proportion to their popular support
questions.explanation: Proportional Representation allocates legislative seats to parties based on the percentage of votes they receive in an election.
questions.hint: 'Proportional' means in proportion to votes received.

questions.question: In a one party State
options: the communist party is the only legal party
the ruling party is the only legal party
questions.explanation: In a one-party state, only one political party is legally permitted to exist and hold power. This is common in some communist and authoritarian regimes.
questions.hint: The name tells you - only ONE party is allowed.

questions.question: A cabinet system of government is practised in
questions.explanation: Britain and Canada operate under the Westminster parliamentary system where the cabinet is drawn from the legislature and is accountable to it.
questions.hint: Think of countries with a Prime Minister and parliamentary system.

questions.question: A party system made up of more than two parties may not qualify for the title of a multi-party system when
options: the country in question has a unitary form of government
the country in question has a federal form of government
different parties are supported by distinct political interests
questions.explanation: For a true multi-party system, parties must be competitive. If one party dominates while others are weak or non-competitive, it functions like a dominant-party system rather than a genuine multi-party system.
questions.hint: Quantity doesn't matter if there's no real competition.

questions.question: A proclamation by the Head of State ending a session of parliament is called
questions.explanation: Prorogation is the formal end of a parliamentary session, typically by the Head of State. Unlike dissolution, it doesn't end the parliament itself but ends the current session.
questions.hint: It ends a session but not the entire parliament.

questions.question: The constitutions of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
options: allows for the dominance of the minority ethnic groups
concentrates governmental power at one level of government
ensures the dominance of one political party
questions.explanation: Nigerian constitutions aim to promote unity among the country's diverse ethnic, religious, and cultural groups through federalism and other unifying mechanisms.
questions.hint: Nigeria has many ethnic groups - the constitution tries to unite them.

questions.question: The transfer of authority to local government council is known as
questions.explanation: Devolution is the statutory transfer of powers and responsibilities from central government to lower levels of government, such as local government councils.
questions.hint: It involves giving power DOWN to lower levels.

questions.question: The principle of anonymity of civil servants means that they
options: are not the servant of a particular government
are trained for the duties they performed
are credited or blamed for anything they do
questions.explanation: Anonymity means civil servants serve the government of the day without personal attribution. They are not identified with any particular political party or government.
questions.hint: They serve whoever is in power without personal political identity.

questions.question: The six registered political parties in Nigeria in 1982 were
options: UPN, NPN, PPA, PPP, NPP and NAP
NPN, UPN, NPP, PRP, PPP, and NNDP
PPA, NCNC, GNPP, NPN, UPN, and PPP
UPN, GNPP, NAP, PRP, NPP and NPN
questions.explanation: During Nigeria's Second Republic (1979-1983), the six registered political parties were: Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN), Great Nigeria People's Party (GNPP), Nigerian Advance Party (NAP), People's Redemption Party (PRP), Nigeria People's Party (NPP), and National Party of Nigeria (NPN).
questions.hint: Remember the major parties of the Second Republic: UPN, NPN, NPP, PRP, GNPP, and NAP.

questions.question: Which if the following is NOT a pressure group in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The Catholic Church is a religious organization, not a pressure group. While it may engage in advocacy, pressure groups specifically aim to influence government policy on particular issues.
questions.hint: Pressure groups advocate for specific professional or economic interests.

questions.question: The N.C.N.C. sent a delegation to London in 1947 to protest against the
questions.explanation: The NCNC (National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons) led by Nnamdi Azikiwe sent a delegation to London in 1947 to protest against the Richards Constitution of 1946, which they felt was imposed without Nigerian consultation.
questions.hint: This constitution was named after Governor Arthur Richards and was opposed by nationalists.

questions.question: A popular principle of colonial administration in British West Africa was
questions.explanation: Indirect rule was the British colonial policy of governing through existing traditional rulers and political structures. It was famously associated with Lord Lugard in Nigeria.
questions.hint: The British governed through traditional chiefs.

questions.question: The three great Nigerian nationalists between 1950 and 1966 were
options: Herbert Macaulay, Nnamdi Azikiwe and Obafemi Awolowo
Ahmadu Bello, Nnamdi Azikiwe and Theophilus Danjuma
Obafemi Awolowo, Herbert Macaulay and Ahmadu Bello
Nnamdi Azikiwe, Obafemi Awolowo and Ahmadu Bello
questions.explanation: The three prominent nationalists during Nigeria's independence struggle were Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe (East), Chief Obafemi Awolowo (West), and Sir Ahmadu Bello (North).
questions.hint: These three leaders represented Nigeria's three major regions.

questions.question: The first Governor-General of colonial Nigeria was
questions.explanation: Lord Frederick Lugard served as the first Governor-General of Nigeria after the amalgamation of Northern and Southern Nigeria in 1914.
questions.hint: He is famous for amalgamating Nigeria in 1914.

questions.question: The first military government in Nigeria was headed by
questions.explanation: Major General Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi led the first military government in Nigeria after the January 15, 1966 coup that overthrew the First Republic.
questions.hint: He took over after the first coup in 1966.

questions.question: The legislature in every state of the Federal Republic of Nigeria is called the
questions.explanation: In Nigeria's federal system, each state has a House of Assembly as its legislative body, while the National Assembly (Senate and House of Representatives) is at the federal level.
questions.hint: State legislators are called 'Honorable' members of the...

questions.question: The 1979 Nigerian Consititution is unique because it
options: provides for a head of government
introduces a participation of women in politics
provides for a head of state who is also head of government
makes all men equal before the law
questions.explanation: The 1979 Constitution introduced the American-style presidential system where the President serves as both Head of State and Head of Government, unlike the parliamentary system under earlier constitutions.

questions.question: The supreme organ of the U.N.O. is the
questions.explanation: The General Assembly is the main deliberative, policymaking, and representative organ of the United Nations, where all member states have equal representation.
questions.hint: All member nations have a seat here.

questions.question: Which Public Commission was not establsihed by the 1979 constitution?
questions.explanation: The Udoji Commission was a Public Service Review Commission established in 1972-1974 to review the civil service, not a commission created by the 1979 Constitution.
questions.hint: This commission is associated with civil service reforms in the 1970s.

questions.question: Local Government Reforms were carried out by the Federal Military Government in
questions.explanation: The major Local Government Reforms in Nigeria were carried out in 1976 under General Olusegun Obasanjo's military government, creating a uniform local government system nationwide.
questions.hint: These reforms happened during the Obasanjo military regime.

questions.question: Lagos became a Crown Colony in
questions.explanation: Lagos was annexed as a British Crown Colony in 1862, marking the beginning of formal British colonial presence in what would later become Nigeria.
questions.hint: This was before the Berlin Conference of 1884-85.

questions.question: Nigerian elites agitated against colonial rule
questions.explanation: Nigerian nationalists used newspapers as a primary tool for political mobilization and agitation against colonial rule. Newspapers like the West African Pilot, founded by Nnamdi Azikiwe, were crucial in spreading nationalist ideas.
questions.hint: The press was the weapon of choice for nationalist mobilization.

questions.question: The first political party in Nigeria was formed after the introduction of the
questions.explanation: The Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP), formed by Herbert Macaulay in 1923, was Nigeria's first political party. It emerged following the Clifford Constitution of 1922 which introduced elective principles.
questions.hint: The 1922 constitution introduced the elective principle.

questions.question: The ECOWAS treaty was signed in 1975 in
questions.explanation: The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) treaty was signed in Lome, Togo, on May 28, 1975.
questions.hint: The treaty was signed in the capital of Togo.

questions.question: The major innovation of the Republican consittution of 1963 was that
options: the Prime Minister ceased to be Head of State
the Governor's office as the representative of the Queen was abolished
the Cabinet was no longer responsible to the legislature
it introduced the Executive Presidential System
questions.explanation: The 1963 Republican Constitution abolished the office of Governor-General (the Queen's representative) and established Nigeria as a republic with a President as ceremonial Head of State.
questions.hint: Nigeria became a republic and removed ties to the British monarchy.

questions.question: Which of these was NOT a demand of the nationalists movements in Nigeria?
options: Promotion of Africans to senior service positions
Increase African representation in the legislative asemblies
Improved conditions of service and salaries for Africa
Withdrawal of Military bases from Africa
questions.explanation: The withdrawal of military bases from Africa was not a primary demand of Nigerian nationalists during the colonial period. Their focus was on self-governance, representation, and improved conditions within Nigeria.
questions.hint: Nationalists were focused on Nigerian-specific issues.

questions.question: Under the 1979 Nigerian Constitution, each state is allowed to elect
options: as many senators as the state can finance
from two to five senators depending on the population of the state
questions.explanation: Under the 1979 Constitution, each state of the federation was entitled to five senators, ensuring equal representation for all states in the Senate regardless of population size.
questions.hint: Each state had the same number of senators.

questions.question: The Independence Constitution
options: provided for a republican status for the country
was imposed on Nigerians by the British
questions.explanation: The Independence Constitution of 1960 was negotiated by Nigerian political leaders at constitutional conferences in London and Lagos, reflecting the transition to self-rule.
questions.hint: It came after years of negotiations.

questions.question: The first general election in Nigeria was held in
questions.explanation: The first general election in Nigeria was held in 1959, preceding independence in 1960. This election was conducted to form the government that would lead Nigeria to independence.
questions.hint: This election happened just before independence.

questions.question: The 1979 Nigerian Constitution provides for Presidential elections every
options: time the military hands over the reins of government
time the incubent is impeached or dies
questions.explanation: The 1979 Constitution established a presidential system with a fixed term of four years for the President, renewable for one additional term.
questions.hint: This is the standard term length for executive presidents in many democracies.

questions.question: In Nigeria, the constitution that preserves civil liberty is the
questions.explanation: Law courts (the judiciary) are the primary protectors of civil liberties, as they interpret the constitution, enforce fundamental rights, and provide remedies for violations of citizens' rights.
questions.hint: Where do citizens go to seek justice when their rights are violated?

questions.question: Nigeria became a Federation under the new constitution of 1954 because the constitution
options: provided for equal representation between the North and the South
created the post of a Prime Minister
provided for a division of members of parliament
provided for a division of functions between the centre and component units
questions.explanation: The 1954 Lyttleton Constitution established Nigeria as a true federation by creating a clear division of functions and powers between the central government and the three regional governments.
questions.hint: Federalism involves sharing power between central and regional governments.

questions.question: The Economic Commission for Africa is an agency of the
questions.explanation: The Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is one of the five regional commissions of the United Nations (UNO), established in 1958 to promote economic development in Africa.
questions.hint: It's a UN regional economic commission.

questions.question: In a presidential system of government the Executive
options: makes laws for the National Assembly
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, the Executive (headed by the President) forms the government, but operates separately from the legislature which makes laws.
questions.hint: The President and their appointed officials constitute the government.

questions.question: A Constitution is a legal document
options: forming the basis upon which a government rules the country
which must not be altered by any succeeding government
questions.explanation: A constitution is the fundamental legal document that establishes the framework for government, defines its powers, and sets out the rights of citizens.
questions.hint: It's the supreme law that establishes how a country is governed.

questions.question: One features of a totalitarian State is the existence of
questions.explanation: A totalitarian state typically features a single recognized political party that monopolizes power, suppressing all opposition and controlling all aspects of society.
questions.hint: Only one party is allowed to exist and hold power.

questions.question: A system in which a few powerful and rich nobles own land which is hired out to the poor people to farm is called
questions.explanation: Feudalism is a social and economic system where nobles own land and grant use to peasants (serfs) in exchange for labor, military service, or rent.
questions.hint: This system was common in medieval Europe with lords, vassals, and serfs.

questions.question: The principle of check and balances is necessary because it
options: makes the Executives stronger than the other organs
leaves each organ of government independent of the Judiciary
questions.explanation: Checks and balances ensure that no single branch of government becomes too powerful or dictatorial by giving each branch some control over the others.
questions.hint: It prevents concentration of power and tyranny.

questions.question: When a constitution is difficult to amend we say it is
questions.explanation: A rigid constitution requires a special procedure for amendment, making it difficult to change compared to ordinary laws.

questions.question: A political authority which maintains sovereign power over a specific geographical area is termed
questions.explanation: The state is a political entity with sovereign power over a defined territory, possessing a government, population, and the capacity to enter into relations with other states.
questions.hint: This entity has four key elements: population, territory, government, and sovereignty.

questions.question: A government in which control of ultimate power is by a few who rule in their own selfish interests is classified as
questions.explanation: Oligarchy is a form of government where a small group of people hold power, often ruling in their own interests rather than for the benefit of all.

questions.question: The most basic property of pressure groups which differentiates them from political parties is that they
options: are not as interested in politics
do not seek to influence public opinion
do not nominate candidates as their own official representatives
questions.explanation: Unlike political parties, pressure groups do not nominate candidates for elections. They seek to influence government policy but do not aim to form a government themselves.
questions.hint: They pressure, but don't present candidates.

questions.question: In a presidential system of government, ministers are
options: individually and collectively responsible to the electorate
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, ministers serve at the pleasure of the president and are individually responsible to him. They can be appointed or dismissed by the president.
questions.hint: They answer directly to the president.

questions.question: The three principal organs of government are the
options: Legistlature, the Public Service and Judiciary
Political Parties, the Executive and the Judiciary
Executive, the Legislature and the Public Corporation
Legislature the Executive and the judiciary
questions.explanation: The three principal organs of government are the Legislature (makes laws), the Executive (implements laws), and the Judiciary (interprets laws).
questions.hint: The three branches that constitute separation of powers.

questions.question: The three FUNDAMENTAL rights of citizens are
options: freedom of movement, association and religion
questions.explanation: The three fundamental rights traditionally recognized are the right to life, liberty, and property. These are considered essential natural rights that governments must protect.
questions.hint: These are considered inalienable natural rights.

questions.question: In a democracy, franchise is given to all
options: citizens except members of the armed forces
questions.explanation: In a democracy, the franchise (right to vote) is granted to qualified adult citizens who meet certain criteria such as age, citizenship, and registration.
questions.hint: Not every adult automatically qualifies - there are requirements.

questions.question: The citizenship of a country may be acquired by an individual through
questions.explanation: Naturalization is the legal process by which a foreign national becomes a citizen of a country, typically after meeting residency and other requirements.
questions.hint: The process of becoming a citizen when you weren't born one.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a public corporation in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The Nigerian National Oil Corporation (NNOC) was later merged to form the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC). It existed but was not a public corporation in the same sense as the others, and eventually became NNPC.
questions.hint: This entity is now known as NNPC.

questions.question: Government means the machinery established by a State to manage the affairs of the
questions.explanation: Government is the institution established by the state to manage public affairs, provide services, and govern the people within its territory.

questions.question: The judicial organ of government is the body which
questions.explanation: The judiciary interprets the law, resolves disputes, and ensures justice is administered according to the constitution and statutes.
questions.hint: Courts and judges interpret and apply the law.

questions.question: A constitutionally defined set of individual rights which governments are obliged to protect constitutes
questions.explanation: Civil rights are the constitutionally protected rights of individuals that governments must respect and protect, such as freedom of speech, religion, and due process.
questions.hint: These are guaranteed by the constitution.

questions.question: An electoral districts is a
questions.explanation: An electoral district, also known as a constituency, is a territorial division from which a representative is elected to a legislative body.
questions.hint: The area represented by one elected official.

questions.question: In a parliamentary system of government, ministers are
options: appointed by a two-thirds majority of the legislature
questions.explanation: In a parliamentary system, ministers are collectively responsible to parliament, meaning they must maintain the confidence of the legislature to remain in office.
questions.hint: They can be removed by a vote of no confidence.

questions.question: A fascist regime is both
questions.explanation: Fascism is characterized by authoritarian rule, dictatorial power, and totalitarian control over all aspects of society, suppressing opposition and individual freedoms.
questions.hint: Think of Mussolini's Italy or Hitler's Germany.

questions.question: Capitalism often encourages
options: public ownership of all forms of enterprises
private ownership of the means of production
deconcentration of political and economic powers in the same hands
questions.explanation: Capitalism is an economic system based on private ownership of the means of production, with individuals and businesses operating for profit in a competitive market.
questions.hint: Private individuals own businesses and property.

questions.question: Citizens legally qualified to vote for parliamentary candidates form
options: members of the House of Assembly
questions.explanation: The electorate refers to the body of citizens who are qualified and registered to vote in elections.

questions.question: Elections among candidates from the same party before the final elections are called
questions.explanation: Primary elections (primaries) are held within political parties to select candidates who will represent the party in the general election.
questions.hint: They happen before the main election.

questions.question: With whom is fascism associated?
questions.explanation: Fascism is primarily associated with Benito Mussolini, who established the first fascist regime in Italy (1922-1943). Adolf Hitler's Nazism was a variant of fascism.
questions.hint: The Italian dictator who coined the term.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT the responsiblitiy of the Federal Electoral Commission in Nigeria?
options: swearing in of members of the Houses of Assembly
questions.explanation: Swearing in of elected members is the responsibility of the judiciary or legislative officials, not the electoral commission. FEDECO (later INEC) handles elections, not inaugurations.
questions.hint: Elections vs. inauguration are different functions.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a civic obligations of every Nigerian citizen?
questions.explanation: Freedom of conscience and religion is a right (not an obligation) enjoyed by citizens. Civic obligations are duties citizens must perform, such as obeying laws, paying taxes, and voting.
questions.hint: Rights vs. duties - which is something you MUST do?

questions.question: The main deliberative organ of the U.N.O. is the
questions.explanation: The General Assembly is the main deliberative organ of the United Nations, where all member states have equal representation and discuss international issues.
questions.hint: All member nations have a seat here.

questions.question: The first black African State to gain political independence from a colonial power was
questions.explanation: Ghana (formerly Gold Coast) gained independence from Britain on March 6, 1957, becoming the first black African country to achieve independence from colonial rule.

questions.question: The major conflict that threatened the existence of the O.A.U. as an international organization from 1982-3 was the conflict
options: between the Polisario Front and Morrocco
questions.explanation: The Western Sahara conflict between the Polisario Front and Morocco deeply divided the OAU, with member states split over recognizing the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic, threatening the organization's unity.
questions.hint: This conflict involved the disputed territory of Western Sahara.

questions.question: Voting first started in Nigeria in 1923 because the Clifford Constitution of 1922 granted
questions.explanation: The Clifford Constitution of 1922 introduced the elective principle, allowing for the election of three Nigerian members to the Legislative Council, marking the beginning of voting in Nigeria.
questions.hint: This allowed Nigerians to elect representatives for the first time.

questions.question: Which organ of the U.N.O. can impose mandatory sanctions on any of its members?
questions.explanation: The Security Council has the primary responsibility for international peace and security and can impose mandatory sanctions under Chapter VII of the UN Charter.
questions.hint: This organ has five permanent members with veto power.

questions.question: A historic feature of the Legislative Council that met in 1923 was that for the first time it
options: included official members who were Nigerians
questions.explanation: The 1923 Legislative Council was historic because it included elected African members for the first time, following the elective principle introduced by the Clifford Constitution of 1922.
questions.hint: Nigerians were now being elected, not just appointed.

questions.question: The Richards' constitution
options: Abolished the elective principle in choosing members of the Legislative Council
amalgamated the northern and southern groups of provinces
abolished the system of indirect rule
questions.explanation: The Richards Constitution of 1946 established a central legislative council for the whole country and introduced regional assemblies, though it was criticized for being imposed without Nigerian consultation.
questions.hint: It created a unified legislative body for Nigeria.

questions.question: The military was last in power in Nigeria between
questions.explanation: The military was last in power from 1966 to 1979, starting with the first coup in January 1966 and ending with the transition to the Second Republic in October 1979.
questions.hint: This period covers the first military interregnum before 1979.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT established by the 1979 Nigerian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The National Universities Commission (NUC) was established in 1962, not by the 1979 Constitution. The 1979 Constitution established several federal commissions including FEDECO, Police Service Commission, and National Population Commission.
questions.hint: This commission was created much earlier for university regulation.

questions.question: To which of these groups did Nigeria belong before the formation of the O.A.U?
questions.explanation: Nigeria belonged to the Monrovia group, a bloc of moderate African states that advocated for gradual decolonization and cooperation with former colonial powers, as opposed to the radical Casablanca group.
questions.hint: This group favored a moderate approach to African unity.

questions.question: The international organization formed after the Second World War to guarantee international peace and security is called
questions.explanation: The United Nations Organization (UNO) was established in 1945 after World War II to maintain international peace and security, replacing the ineffective League of Nations.

questions.question: The two parties which formed the coalition government in 1959 were the
questions.explanation: After the 1959 elections, the Northern People's Congress (NPC) formed a coalition government with the National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC) to form Nigeria's first independent government.
questions.hint: The coalition brought together the North and East.

questions.question: The 1953 motion that Nigeria should become independent in 1956 was moved by
questions.explanation: Chief Anthony Enahoro moved the motion for self-government in 1953, calling for Nigeria's independence in 1956. The motion sparked significant debate and controversy.
questions.hint: He was from the Western Region and moved the historic motion.

questions.question: Southern Nigeria was divided into Eastern and Western Provinces for administrative purposes in
questions.explanation: In 1939, Governor Sir Bernard Bourdillon divided Southern Nigeria into Eastern and Western Provinces for administrative efficiency.
questions.hint: This division happened just before World War II.

questions.question: Under what constitution did the Supreme Court become the highest Court of Appeal in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The 1963 Republican Constitution made the Supreme Court the highest court of appeal in Nigeria, replacing the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in London.
questions.hint: This constitution made Nigeria a republic.

questions.question: The Constitution which introduced the ministerial system into the Nigerian Political system is the
questions.explanation: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 introduced the ministerial system, creating regional governments with ministers responsible to regional legislatures.
questions.hint: This constitution established true federalism and ministers.

questions.question: The Minorities Commission appointed in Nigeria in 1957 recommended that
options: more states should be created in the Federation
no more states should be created before independence
the Federal Legislature should legislate for the minority areas
all the minority areas should constitute one state
questions.explanation: The Minorities Commission (Willink Commission) recommended that no more states should be created before independence, instead proposing safeguards for minority groups within the existing regions.
questions.hint: The commission advised against creating additional states at that time.

questions.question: The second military coup d'etat in Nigeria took place on
questions.explanation: The second military coup occurred on July 29, 1966, when a group of northern officers overthrew General Aguiyi-Ironsi's government, leading to General Yakubu Gowon assuming power.
questions.hint: This was the counter-coup following the January 1966 coup.

questions.question: To be elected President of Nigeria, the 1979 Constitution states that one must have attained the age of
questions.explanation: The 1979 Constitution required a presidential candidate to be at least 35 years old. This age requirement was maintained in subsequent constitutions.
questions.hint: The same age requirement applies today for Nigerian presidency.

questions.question: Which of the following international organisations was in exisitence before the outbrteak of the Second World War?
questions.explanation: The League of Nations was established in 1919 after World War I and existed until 1946, making it the only organization listed that predates World War II (1939-1945).
questions.hint: This was the precursor to the United Nations.

questions.question: Rates are generally collected in Nigeria by
questions.explanation: Local Government Councils are responsible for collecting rates (property taxes) within their jurisdictions as part of their revenue generation functions.
questions.hint: The tier of government closest to the people collects rates.

questions.question: An electoral district for a local government election is a
questions.explanation: A ward is the smallest electoral district used for local government elections, with each ward electing a councilor to the local government council.
questions.hint: This is the unit that elects a local government councilor.

questions.question: All the following are organs of the O.A.U. EXCEPT
options: The commission on Mediation, Conciliation and Arbitration
questions.explanation: The Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is a UN agency, not an organ of the OAU (now African Union). The OAU's main organs included the Assembly of Heads of State, Council of Ministers, General Secretariat, and the Commission on Mediation, Conciliation and Arbitration.
questions.hint: This is a UN regional commission, not an OAU organ.

questions.question: Public opinion is important because it
options: tells government what action it must take
lets government know what the people want
allows the police to determine trouble makers
questions.explanation: Public opinion serves as a channel for citizens to communicate their needs, preferences, and concerns to government, helping ensure responsiveness to the people.
questions.hint: It acts as a feedback mechanism from citizens to government.

questions.question: Which branch of government is responsible for implementing laws? The
questions.explanation: The Executive branch implements and enforces laws, while the Legislature makes laws and the Judiciary interprets them.
questions.hint: The President and their cabinet execute laws.

questions.question: Universal Adult Suffrage means all
questions.explanation: Universal Adult Suffrage means that all adult citizens (typically 18 years and above) have the right to vote, regardless of property, education, gender, or other qualifications.
questions.hint: Every adult citizen has the voting right.

questions.question: In a democarcy, sovereignty is vested in
questions.explanation: In a democracy, sovereignty rests with the people (the community). Popular sovereignty means the people are the ultimate source of political authority.
questions.hint: The people are the ultimate source of power.

questions.question: A bill that applies to the whole population and is intended to promote the general welfare is called
questions.explanation: A public bill affects the general population and deals with matters of public policy, as opposed to private bills which affect specific individuals or groups.
questions.hint: This type of bill affects everyone in the country.

questions.question: The rule of law implies
options: that only the Head of State is above the law
that no one is above the law
that only the National Assembly can make laws
questions.explanation: The rule of law means that all persons, institutions, and entities are accountable to laws that are publicly promulgated, equally enforced, and independently adjudicated.
questions.hint: Everyone, including government officials, must obey the law.

questions.question: A one party system of government
options: does not provide for a legislature
does not accept the doctrine of separation of powers
questions.explanation: In a one-party system, only one political party is legally permitted to exist, and there is no official opposition party, as opposition is suppressed or banned.
questions.hint: There is no alternative party to challenge the ruling party.

questions.question: A constitution is classified as unwritten because it
options: does not emanate from the legislature
is not contained in any one document
questions.explanation: An unwritten constitution is not contained in a single document but is derived from multiple sources including statutes, conventions, and judicial precedents, as in the United Kingdom.
questions.hint: It's scattered across multiple sources, not one book.

questions.question: Delegated legislation is the power to make laws by
options: local councils when parliament is recess
questions.explanation: Delegated legislation (or secondary legislation) occurs when parliament delegates law-making powers to other bodies such as ministers, local authorities, or regulatory agencies.
questions.hint: Parliament gives authority to others to make specific rules.

questions.question: In a unitary system of government
options: there is a high degree of centralization
there is no separation of powers
legislative powers cannot be delegated to local councils
questions.explanation: A unitary system concentrates power in the central government, with local governments exercising only powers delegated to them by the center.
questions.hint: Power is concentrated at the national level.

questions.question: Case-laws are made by the
questions.explanation: Case law (or common law) is law developed by judges through decisions made in court cases, establishing precedents for future cases.
questions.hint: Courts create these through judicial decisions.

questions.question: The theory of separation of powers was for the first time clearly formulated by
questions.explanation: Baron de Montesquieu (1689-1755), a French political philosopher, clearly formulated the theory of separation of powers in his work 'The Spirit of the Laws' (1748).
questions.hint: French philosopher who greatly influenced the US Constitution.

questions.question: The deliberate tampering with the delimitation of constituencies in order to win more seat is called
questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the manipulation of electoral district boundaries to favor a particular political party or group, often by creating odd-shaped districts.
questions.hint: Named after American politician Elbridge Gerry.

questions.question: Fascism developed in
questions.explanation: Fascism first developed in Italy under Benito Mussolini in the 1920s, though it later spread to Germany (Nazism) and other European countries.
questions.hint: Mussolini's Italy was the birthplace of fascism.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good example of a confederal state?
questions.explanation: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) is a confederation where sovereign states voluntarily unite for specific purposes while retaining their sovereignty.
questions.hint: Think of an organization where member states remain fully independent.

questions.question: In a simple majority electoral system, the candidate that wins is the one who
options: obtains the greatest number of votes cast
has spent the most amount of money
has travelled most widely in the country
has most support among labour union leaders
questions.explanation: In a simple majority (first-past-the-post) system, the candidate who receives the highest number of votes wins, regardless of whether they achieve an absolute majority.
questions.hint: The person with the most votes wins.

questions.question: An election conducted to fill a vacant post in a legislature is known as
questions.explanation: A by-election (or bye-election) is held to fill a political office that has become vacant between general elections, typically due to death, resignation, or disqualification.
questions.hint: Held to fill a single vacancy.

questions.question: Constitutionalism means
options: the constitution is largely made up of conventions
the constitution is not easy to amend
the provisions of the constitution are strictly adhered to
questions.explanation: Constitutionalism is the principle that government authority is derived from and limited by a body of fundamental law, ensuring that constitutional provisions are respected and followed.
questions.hint: Government operates according to constitutional limits.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a member of the Judiciary?
questions.explanation: A lawyer is a legal practitioner but is not a member of the judiciary unless appointed as a judge or magistrate. The judiciary consists of judges, magistrates, and judicial officers.
questions.hint: Lawyers practice law but don't serve on the bench.

questions.question: A person who is disenfranchised is
options: allowed to excersie his voting right
questions.explanation: Disenfranchisement means the deprivation of the right to vote, either as a punishment for a crime or due to other legal restrictions.
questions.hint: The prefix 'dis-' means removal of the right.

questions.question: The head of the executive branch in a parliamentary system is called the
questions.explanation: In a parliamentary system, the Prime Minister is the head of government and leads the executive branch, while the Head of State (monarch or president) is often ceremonial.
questions.hint: The leader of the majority party in parliament.

questions.question: The political neutrality of civil servants implies that they
options: are not allowed to join any organization or group
are not allowed to be involved in partisan politics
questions.explanation: Political neutrality means civil servants must remain impartial and not participate in partisan political activities, allowing them to serve any government regardless of its political affiliation.
questions.hint: They serve the government of the day without political bias.

questions.question: The supreme power of a state to make and enforce laws within its jurisdiction is called
questions.explanation: Sovereignty is the supreme authority of a state to govern itself, make laws, and exercise control within its territory without external interference.
questions.hint: The ultimate authority within a territory.

questions.question: Ceremonial and executive powers are usually fused in a
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, the president serves as both Head of State (ceremonial) and Head of Government (executive), fusing both roles in one office.
questions.hint: The same person is both head of state and head of government.

questions.question: The members of the Nigerian Constituent Assembly were elected in 1983 by
questions.explanation: The members of the Constituent Assembly that drafted the 1979 Constitution were elected during the general elections of 1978, not 1983. The 1983 elections were general elections for the Second Republic.
questions.hint: Constituent Assembly members were chosen in the elections that ushered in the Second Republic.

questions.question: The name of the president of the Nigerian Labour Congress elected in 1983 is
questions.explanation: Alhaji Hassan Sunmonu was elected as the President of the Nigerian Labour Congress (NLC) in 1983, serving as a prominent labor leader in Nigeria.
questions.hint: He was a prominent labor leader in Nigeria during that period.

questions.question: Under the 1979 Nigeria Constitution, the supreme court judges were appointed by the
questions.explanation: Under the 1979 Constitution, Supreme Court judges were appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Judicial Service Commission, subject to confirmation by the Senate.

questions.question: The annual budget of the O.A.U. is approved by the
options: Assembly of Heads of State and Government
questions.explanation: The Council of Ministers of the OAU (now African Union) was responsible for approving the organization's annual budget, while the Assembly of Heads of State and Government provided overall policy direction.
questions.hint: This body handles administrative and budgetary matters.

questions.question: Lagos was first amalgamated with the Western Region as a result of the
questions.explanation: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 amalgamated Lagos with the Western Region as part of Nigeria's federal structure, making Lagos part of the Western Region until it became a separate federal territory in 1976.
questions.hint: This constitution established true federalism in Nigeria.

questions.question: One of the non-permanent members of the United Nations Security Council is
questions.explanation: Nigeria is a non-permanent member of the UN Security Council, elected for two-year terms, while Britain, USA, and USSR (now Russia) are permanent members with veto power.
questions.hint: Non-permanent members are elected for two-year terms.

questions.question: Local government in Nigeria are created in order to
options: encourage competitions and rivalry among groups
bring the government nearer to the people
questions.explanation: Local governments are created to bring government closer to the people, ensuring grassroots participation, effective service delivery, and local development.
questions.hint: They are the tier of government closest to citizens.

questions.question: The amalgamation of the Northern and Southern Protectorates of Nigeria was in
questions.explanation: The amalgamation of Northern and Southern Nigeria took place in 1914 under Governor Lord Frederick Lugard, creating the Colony and Protectorate of Nigeria.
questions.hint: This happened under Lord Lugard's administration.

questions.question: ECOWAS is
options: an international organ of the United Nations
questions.explanation: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) is a regional economic organization established in 1975 to promote economic integration and development among West African states.
questions.hint: It promotes trade and economic cooperation in West Africa.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a member of ECOWAS
questions.explanation: Cameroon (Cameroun) is not a member of ECOWAS. Cameroon belongs to the Economic Community of Central African States (ECCAS), while Nigeria, Togo, and Ghana are West African members of ECOWAS.
questions.hint: This country is in Central Africa, not West Africa.

questions.question: The Lyttleton Constitution is important because it
options: confirmed that Nigeria would be independent in 1960
abolished the principle of indirect rule
questions.explanation: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 established Nigeria as a true federation with a clear division of powers between the central and regional governments.
questions.hint: It created a proper federal system.

questions.question: The Macpherson Constitution of Nigeia created a
options: bicameral legislature for the Central government
questions.explanation: The Macpherson Constitution of 1951 created a bicameral legislature for the Western Region, consisting of a House of Chiefs and a House of Assembly.
questions.hint: Western Region had two legislative chambers.

questions.question: The Arthur Richards constitution introduced into Nigeria
questions.explanation: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced regionalism by dividing Nigeria into three regions (North, East, West) with separate regional assemblies.
questions.hint: It created regional divisions for administration.

questions.question: In 1966, the Military intervened in Nigeria politics because
options: the country was not operating a presidential system of government
the number of legislature was too large
there was a high level of corruption in the country
the political processes had broken down
questions.explanation: The military intervened in 1966 citing a breakdown of law and order, political instability, corruption, and the failure of the political class to manage the country effectively.
questions.hint: The First Republic collapsed due to political crises.

questions.question: The Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) of the pre-independence era
options: dominated the political scene in Lagos
was formed to replace the Action Group as the ruling party in the Western Region
had branches all over the country
was warmly supported by traditional rulers
questions.explanation: The NNDP, founded by Herbert Macaulay in 1923, was Nigeria's first political party and dominated Lagos politics during the 1920s and 1930s.
questions.hint: This party was based in Lagos.

questions.question: The Clifford Constitution
options: made Governor dependent on the Executive Council
provided for an unofficial majority membership of the Legislature Council
introduced the elective principle into Nigerian politics
questions.explanation: The Clifford Constitution of 1922 introduced the elective principle, allowing for the election of three Nigerian members to the Legislative Council.
questions.hint: It allowed Nigerians to elect representatives for the first time.

questions.question: Under the Independence Constitution
options: the governor-general could appoint any member of the House of Representatives as a prime minister
a bicameral legislature was introduced in the country
forty-four members were elected into the Senate
the supreme court of Nigeria was made the highest judicial authority in the country
questions.explanation: The Independence Constitution of 1960 established a bicameral legislature at the federal level, consisting of the Senate (upper house) and the House of Representatives (lower house).
questions.hint: Nigeria had both a Senate and House of Representatives.

questions.question: Which of the following nations does not have veto power in the Security Council of the UNO?
questions.explanation: Germany does not have veto power in the UN Security Council. The five permanent members (P5) with veto power are the United States, Russia (formerly USSR), United Kingdom, France, and China.
questions.hint: Only the P5 have veto power.

questions.question: Public corporations are established mainly to
options: cater for the welfare of their board members
give advice to the government on commerce
co-ordinate the affairs of several amenities
provide essential services and amenities on a commercial basis
questions.explanation: Public corporations are established to provide essential services (electricity, water, transportation, etc.) efficiently and on a commercial or semi-commercial basis.

questions.question: The first military coup d'etat in Nigeria occurred on
questions.explanation: The first military coup in Nigeria occurred on January 15, 1966, led by Major Chukwuma Kaduna Nzeogwu and other young military officers, overthrowing the First Republic.

questions.question: Nigeria adopted a republican constitution on
questions.explanation: Nigeria adopted a republican constitution on October 1, 1963, becoming a republic within the Commonwealth, with the Queen of England no longer as Head of State.
questions.hint: Nigeria became a republic three years after independence.

questions.question: The folowing six political parties were registered for the 1983 elections
options: NPN, NAP, NPP, PRP, GNPP, UPN
NPN, NNA, PPP, PRP, PPA, UPN
UPN, AG, NNDP, PRP, NAP, PPA
questions.explanation: The six political parties registered for the 1983 elections were: National Party of Nigeria (NPN), Nigerian Advance Party (NAP), Nigeria People's Party (NPP), People's Redemption Party (PRP), Great Nigeria People's Party (GNPP), and Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN).
questions.hint: These were the parties of the Second Republic.

questions.question: In Nigeria, the office of Prime Minister was first created in
questions.explanation: The office of Prime Minister was first created in 1954 under the Lyttleton Constitution, with the establishment of a federal system and regional governments.
questions.hint: This constitution created the position of Prime Minister.

questions.question: The non-permanent members of the Security Council of the United Nations Organisations (UNO) are elected for
questions.explanation: Non-permanent members of the UN Security Council are elected for two-year terms by the General Assembly, with five members elected each year.
questions.hint: They serve for a short renewable term.

questions.question: The Economic Commission for Africa is an organ of
questions.explanation: The Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is one of the five regional commissions of the United Nations (UNO), established to promote economic development in Africa.

questions.question: Aristocracy is the system of government in which the few rule for
questions.explanation: Aristocracy is rule by the few (the 'best' or nobility) for the benefit of all. When the few rule for their own selfish interests, it becomes oligarchy.
questions.hint: Aristos means 'best' in Greek - they rule for the common good.

questions.question: Aristocracy is the system of government in which the few rule for
questions.explanation: Aristocracy is rule by the few (the 'best' or nobility) for the benefit of all. When the few rule for their own selfish interests, it becomes oligarchy.
questions.hint: Aristos means 'best' in Greek - they rule for the common good.

questions.question: The two primary elements in politics are
questions.explanation: Politics fundamentally involves the interplay between order (stability, structure, governance) and conflict (competition, disagreement, resolution of competing interests).
questions.hint: Politics is about managing competing interests.

questions.question: A nation state is synonymous with a
questions.explanation: A nation-state is a sovereign state that is based on a shared national identity, combining the concepts of a nation (people with common identity) and a state (sovereign political entity).
questions.hint: It has supreme authority over its territory.

questions.question: Legislative supremacy exists in
questions.explanation: Legislative supremacy exists in Britain under the Westminster system, where Parliament is sovereign and can make or unmake any law without judicial review.
questions.hint: Parliament is supreme in this country.

questions.question: Presidentialism is a system of government in which
options: there is elected head of State who exercises actual executive powers
the head of State is not the chief executive
the executive functions are the responsiblity of the entire members of a cabinet
all members of cabinet must also be members of the legislature
questions.explanation: Presidentialism is a system where an elected president serves as both Head of State and Head of Government, exercising actual executive powers independently of the legislature.
questions.hint: The president is both ceremonial and executive leader.

questions.question: The main function of the upper chamber in a parliamentary system of government is to
options: acts as a check on the lower chamber
direct the activities of the lower chamber
questions.explanation: The upper chamber (like the House of Lords or Senate) serves primarily as a revising and checking chamber, reviewing legislation passed by the lower house and providing scrutiny.
questions.hint: It reviews and revises bills from the lower house.

questions.question: The federal constitution which concedes to the components units the right to secede is that of
questions.explanation: The Soviet Union's constitution nominally granted republics the right to secede, though in practice this was never exercised until the dissolution of the USSR in 1991.
questions.hint: This country's constitution had a formal secession provision.

questions.question: The principle of two levels of government in a country is called
questions.explanation: Federalism is a system of government where power is divided between a central (federal) government and regional (state or provincial) governments, each with constitutionally defined powers.
questions.hint: Power is shared between national and regional governments.

questions.question: In a cabinet system, the executive is appointed from the
questions.explanation: In a cabinet (parliamentary) system, the executive (cabinet ministers) are appointed from the legislature, typically from the majority party or coalition in parliament.
questions.hint: Ministers are also members of parliament.

questions.question: An important advantage of creating more constituents units in a federal state is to
options: enhance the people's participation in government
enable ambitious politicians gain political power
make the state gain more power
curb the powers of the federal government
questions.explanation: Creating more constituent units (states/provinces) brings government closer to the people, enhances grassroots participation, and allows for more localized governance and development.
questions.hint: It brings government closer to citizens.

questions.question: Under the presidential system
options: the party with the majority of seats forms the Executive
there is the principle of collective responsibility
the president may come from any of the parties
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, the president is directly elected and can come from any political party, regardless of which party controls the legislature.
questions.hint: The president is elected separately from the legislature.

questions.question: The major function of the legislative assembly is to
questions.explanation: The primary function of a legislative assembly is lawmaking (legislation), though it also performs representation, oversight, and other functions.

questions.question: In a parliamentary system who ensures that members are in the House to vote on major issues? The
questions.explanation: The Whip is a party official responsible for ensuring party members attend legislative sessions and vote according to party positions on important issues.
questions.hint: They 'whip' members into line for votes.

questions.question: A system in which no single person serves as the chief executive is known as
questions.explanation: A collegial system (or collective executive) is one where executive power is vested in a group or committee rather than a single individual, such as the Swiss Federal Council.
questions.hint: Power is shared among a group of equals.

questions.question: The idea of democracy was first associated with the
questions.explanation: The idea of democracy originated in ancient Greece, particularly in Athens around the 5th century BCE, where citizens participated directly in decision-making.

questions.question: According to Marxist theory, those who live by selling their labour are referred to as the
questions.explanation: In Marxist theory, the proletariat (proletarians) are the working class who sell their labor for wages, as distinguished from the bourgeoisie who own the means of production.
questions.hint: These are the workers who sell their labor.

questions.question: A social system in which power is derived from control over land is called
questions.explanation: Feudalism is a social system where political and economic power is derived from control over land, with lords owning land and vassals/peasants working it in exchange for protection.
questions.hint: Land ownership equals power in this system.

questions.question: A system of government in which power derives from total control of the instruments of force is called
questions.explanation: Fascism is characterized by total control of military and police forces to maintain power, suppressing opposition through force and coercion.
questions.hint: This ideology relies heavily on military and police power.

questions.question: Which of the following ideologies emphasizes the abolition of goverment and law?
questions.explanation: Anarchism advocates for the abolition of all forms of government and law, believing in a stateless society based on voluntary cooperation.
questions.hint: This ideology wants no government at all.

questions.question: The writ of habeas corpus is applied to secure the
questions.explanation: Habeas corpus (Latin for 'you shall have the body') is a legal writ that protects against unlawful detention, securing the right to personal liberty by requiring a court to review the legality of imprisonment.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental human right in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Right to education is not a fundamental human right in the Nigerian constitution. It is a social and economic right rather than a fundamental right. Fundamental rights include right to life, liberty, freedom of thought, conscience, and religion.
questions.hint: This is a socio-economic right, not fundamental.

questions.question: Proportional representation is recommended because it
questions.explanation: Proportional representation allows smaller parties to gain representation in proportion to their vote share, making it more favorable to minority parties.
questions.hint: It gives smaller parties a chance at representation.

questions.question: A system of voting in which the voters are asked a 'yes' or 'no' question on a major issue is called
questions.explanation: A referendum is a direct vote in which citizens are asked to approve or reject a specific proposal, policy, or constitutional amendment with a 'yes' or 'no' vote.
questions.hint: Citizens vote directly on an issue.

questions.question: A political manifesto is a document which outlines
questions.explanation: A political manifesto is a document published by a political party outlining its policies, principles, and program of action for governance.
questions.hint: It states what a party promises to do if elected.

questions.question: One argument against a multi-party system is the
options: encouragement of diverse opinion and opposition
questions.explanation: Critics argue that multi-party systems can lead to political instability, coalition governments that are weak, and inability to pursue consistent development policies due to frequent changes.
questions.hint: It can lead to unstable coalitions and policy inconsistency.

questions.question: Pre-colonial Igbo society was
questions.explanation: Pre-colonial Igbo society was acephalous (headless), meaning it had no centralized authority or single ruler. Governance was based on village assemblies, age grades, and title societies.
questions.hint: It had no central king or chief.

questions.question: Which of the following ethnic groups had centralised administration in pre-colonial Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The Hausa had centralized administration with the Emirate system, featuring a hierarchical structure with the Emir as the supreme political and religious authority.

questions.question: In which of the following ethnic groups was pre-colonial political structure NOT infuenced by the Islamic culture?
questions.explanation: The Tiv people were not significantly influenced by Islamic culture in their pre-colonial political structure, maintaining their traditional acephalous system.
questions.hint: This group in the Middle Belt maintained traditional religion.

questions.question: The British Government revoked the charter of the Royal Niger Company and took over the direct administration of Nigeria in
questions.explanation: In 1900, the British government revoked the Royal Niger Company's charter and took over direct administration, establishing the Protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria.
questions.hint: This was when the British government took direct control.

questions.question: The Minorities Commission appointed in 1957 was headed by
questions.explanation: The Minorities Commission (Willink Commission) was appointed in 1957 and headed by Sir Henry Willink to investigate minority fears and make recommendations on state creation.
questions.hint: The commission is named after its chairman.

questions.question: The Zikist Movement was popular for its
questions.explanation: The Zikist Movement, named after Nnamdi Azikiwe, was known for its militant nationalism, employing radical and confrontational tactics in the struggle for independence.
questions.hint: They were known for radical activism.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of factors contrbuted to the development of nationalism in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Racial discrimination and oppression under colonial rule, including unequal treatment of Africans and denial of political rights, were primary factors that fueled nationalist movements.

questions.question: Which constitution was created to legislate for the Lagos Colony and the Southern Provinces?
questions.explanation: The Clifford Constitution of 1922 was created to legislate for the Lagos Colony and the Southern Provinces, establishing a Legislative Council for these areas.
questions.hint: This constitution applied to Lagos and the South only.

questions.question: Judges in Nigeria enjoys security of tenure
options: if they are appointed by the president
if they have the support of the Nigerian Bar Association
if they are of good behaviour
during the life of the government which appoints them
questions.explanation: Judges in Nigeria enjoy security of tenure, meaning they can only be removed for misconduct or inability to perform their functions, ensuring judicial independence.
questions.hint: They serve until retirement unless removed for cause.

questions.question: Before the 1963 Constitution, the highest court of appeal for Nigeria was the
questions.explanation: Before the 1963 Republican Constitution, the highest court of appeal for Nigeria was the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in London, which was replaced by the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the Police Force in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Making laws is a function of the legislature, not the police. The police enforce laws, control traffic, arrest criminals, and assist in prosecution.

questions.question: In Nigeria, the highest court for muslims is the
questions.explanation: The Sharia Court of Appeal is the highest court for Muslims in Nigeria on matters of Islamic personal law, though its decisions can be appealed to the Supreme Court.
questions.hint: This court handles Islamic law matters.

questions.question: Who was appointed by the federal government of Nigeria as the administrator of western region in 1962?
questions.explanation: Dr. Moses Adekoyejo Majekodunmi was appointed as Administrator of the Western Region in 1962 following a state of emergency declared due to political crisis.
questions.hint: He was a medical doctor and federal minister.

questions.question: The head of state in the first republic of Nigeria was
options: a nominee of the whole country
questions.explanation: In the First Republic (1960-1966), Nigeria had a parliamentary system with a ceremonial President (nominal head of state) and a Prime Minister as head of government.
questions.hint: The President was ceremonial, not executive.

questions.question: Which of the folowing parties formed the opposition in the House of Representatives during Nigerian first republic
questions.explanation: During the First Republic, the Action Group (AG) and United Middle Belt Congress (UMBC) formed the official opposition in the House of Representatives.
questions.hint: AG was the main opposition party.

questions.question: The main objectives of the Public Service Review Commission headed by Chief Jerome Udoji was to make the Nigerian public service
questions.explanation: The Udoji Commission (1972-1974) aimed to make the Nigerian public service more efficient, productive, and result-oriented through reforms including performance-based pay.
questions.hint: It focused on productivity and efficiency.

questions.question: Local governments in Nigeria receive the bulk of their financial resources from
questions.explanation: Local governments in Nigeria receive the majority of their funding from the Federation Account through allocations from the federal government, supplemented by internally generated revenue.
questions.hint: They depend heavily on federal allocations.

questions.question: The term 'Chief-in-Council,' in Nigeria means that the Chief
options: cannot oppose the decision of the council
questions.explanation: Chief-in-Council means the traditional ruler must act on the advice of his council and cannot oppose its decisions; he rules with but not independently of the council.
questions.hint: He must follow the council's advice.

questions.question: Traditional rulers under the 1976 Local Government Reforms had
questions.explanation: Under the 1976 Local Government Reforms, traditional rulers were given Chief-in-Council status, meaning they had advisory roles but no executive or legislative powers.
questions.hint: They became advisory rather than executive.

questions.question: Nigeria is NOT a member of
questions.explanation: Nigeria is not a member of NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization), which is a military alliance of North American and European countries. Nigeria is a member of the Commonwealth, OPEC, and ECOWAS.
questions.hint: This is a Western military alliance.

questions.question: The greatest achievement of nationalist movement in Africa is the
options: love and peace among African States
questions.explanation: The greatest achievement of African nationalist movements was the attainment of political independence and sovereignty for most African states from colonial rule.
questions.hint: They achieved freedom from colonial powers.

questions.question: The administrative headquarters of O.A.U. is in
questions.explanation: The headquarters of the Organization of African Unity (OAU), now the African Union (AU), is located in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia.

questions.question: The World Health Organisation is an agency of
questions.explanation: The World Health Organization (WHO) is a specialized agency of the United Nations (UNO) responsible for international public health.

questions.question: The organ of the United Nations primarily responsible for maintaining international peace and security is the
questions.explanation: The Security Council has primary responsibility under the UN Charter for maintaining international peace and security, with powers including sanctions and peacekeeping operations.
questions.hint: This organ has the power to authorize force.

questions.question: Which of these groups of African leaders is closely associated with the formation of the O.A.U.?
options: Haile Selassie, Abubakar Tafwa Balewa, William Tubman
Abubakar Tafawa Balewa, Jean Bokassa, Kwame Nkrumah
Kwame Nkrumah, Nmamdi Azikwe, Jomo Kenyatta
Julius Nyerere, Kwame Nkrumah, Haille Selassie
questions.explanation: Julius Nyerere (Tanzania), Kwame Nkrumah (Ghana), and Haile Selassie (Ethiopia) were key founding fathers of the Organization of African Unity (OAU) in 1963.
questions.hint: These three were prominent pan-Africanist leaders.

questions.question: A confederal system of government means
options: strong regional governments and a weak central authority
a strong central authority and weak regional governments
a strong central authority and strong regional governments
a weak central authority and weak regional governments
questions.explanation: In a confederal system, regional governments retain most power and sovereignty, while the central authority is weak and exists only at the pleasure of the constituent units.
questions.hint: Regions are strong, center is weak.

questions.question: The organs of government which are normally fused in a military regime are the
questions.explanation: In a military regime, the executive and legislative powers are fused, with the military council acting as both the law-making and law-executing body.
questions.hint: The military council both makes and enforces laws.

questions.question: In a monarchical form of Government, sovereignty resides with
questions.explanation: In a monarchy, sovereignty resides with the monarch (royalty), who holds supreme authority, either absolute or ceremonial depending on the type of monarchy.
questions.hint: The king or queen holds sovereign power.

questions.question: A constitution is rigid if it
options: is found only in one written document
is changed only by judicial interpretation
questions.explanation: A rigid constitution requires special procedures for amendment that are more difficult than the ordinary legislative process.

questions.question: In a unitary state, power is concentrated in the
questions.explanation: In a unitary system, sovereignty and power are concentrated in the central (national) government, which may delegate powers to local units but can also revoke them.
questions.hint: The central government holds supreme authority.

questions.question: Fundamental rights are best guaranteed by
questions.explanation: An independent judiciary is essential for protecting fundamental rights, as courts can declare laws unconstitutional and provide remedies for rights violations.

questions.question: The main objective of pressure groups is to
options: mobilize support on behalf of government
protect the interests of its members
questions.explanation: Pressure groups aim to protect and promote the specific interests of their members by influencing government policy, rather than seeking to win political power themselves.
questions.hint: They advocate for their members' interests.

questions.question: Franchise means
questions.explanation: Franchise (or suffrage) refers to the right to vote in political elections.
questions.hint: It's the right to cast a ballot.

questions.question: A parliamentary system of government is charcterized by
