questions.question: The Obasanjo administration nationalized the assets of the British Petroleum over the British government's policy in
questions.explanation: The Obasanjo administration nationalized British Petroleum (BP) assets in 1979 due to the British government's policy towards apartheid Rhodesia (now Zimbabwe) and its recognition of the illegal Smith regime.

questions.question: Nigeria foreign policy implementation is the responsibility of the
questions.explanation: The Ministry of External Affairs (now Ministry of Foreign Affairs) is responsible for implementing Nigeria's foreign policy through diplomatic missions abroad.

questions.question: The head of a Nigerian mission in a Commonwealth country is called
questions.explanation: In Commonwealth countries, diplomatic missions are called High Commissions and the head is a High Commissioner (not Ambassador, which is used for non-Commonwealth countries).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a statutory function of an embassy?
questions.explanation: Espionage (spying) is not a statutory function of an embassy - it is illegal under international law. Embassies legally issue visas, collect open information, and protect citizens' welfare.
questions.hint: Spying is not legal embassy work.

questions.question: ECOWAS is aimed at uniting the West African states
questions.explanation: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) primarily aims to unite West African states economically through trade liberalization, common currency, and economic integration.

questions.question: Which of these following countries pioneered the idea of ECOWAS alongside Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Togo, under President Gnassingbé Eyadéma, pioneered the idea of ECOWAS alongside Nigeria's General Yakubu Gowon in the early 1970s.

questions.question: Which of these countries was NOT a foundation member of the Organisation of Africa Unity?
questions.explanation: Zimbabwe was not a foundation member of the OAU (1963) because it was still under white minority rule (Rhodesia) and did not gain independence until 1980.

questions.question: The United Nations was founded with the primary objective of
options: uniting all nations into a world federation
questions.explanation: The United Nations was founded primarily to maintain international peace and security, preventing future wars and promoting peaceful resolution of disputes.

questions.question: Until her independence, Namibia was a
questions.explanation: Until independence in 1990, Namibia (formerly South West Africa) was a United Nations trust territory, though illegally administered by South Africa.
questions.hint: UN trust territory under South African control.

questions.question: A political authority which exercises sovereign power over a specific geographical area is termed a
questions.explanation: A state is a political entity with sovereign power over a defined territory, possessing a government, population, and the capacity to enter into relations with other states.
questions.hint: Has territory, population, government, and sovereignty.

questions.question: The process by which political beliefs and values are transmitted from one generation to another is best known as political
questions.explanation: Political socialization is the lifelong process through which individuals acquire political beliefs, values, attitudes, and behaviors from family, school, media, and other agents.
questions.hint: How we learn politics from childhood.

questions.question: The fusion of the executive and legislative organs of government is associated with
questions.explanation: The parliamentary system features fusion of powers, where the executive (cabinet) is drawn from and accountable to the legislature, blending these branches.
questions.hint: Prime Minister and cabinet are also members of parliament.

questions.question: One criticism of delegated legislation is that it
options: is not useful in emergency situation
questions.explanation: Delegated legislation is criticized because it subjects citizens to executive-made laws without full parliamentary scrutiny, potentially undermining democratic accountability.
questions.hint: Executive makes laws without full legislative debate.

questions.question: A system of government in which the central government is supreme over other levels is
questions.explanation: A unitary system concentrates power in the central government, which may delegate authority to local units but can also withdraw it.

questions.question: Which of the following defines a constitution?
options: Basic laws for the conduct of modern government
The doctrine that limits the powers of government
questions.explanation: A constitution is the fundamental set of basic laws and principles that establish the framework for government, define its powers, and set out the rights of citizens.
questions.hint: The supreme law that establishes how a country is governed.

questions.question: Which of the following ideologies advocates governmental control over all sources of powers?
questions.explanation: Totalitarianism advocates complete government control over all aspects of public and private life, including all sources of power.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an acceptable means of achieving democracy?
questions.explanation: Riots are not an acceptable means of achieving democracy as they involve violence and lawlessness, unlike referendum, recall, and initiative which are peaceful democratic processes.

questions.question: In the cabinet system of government, individual responsibility of ministers means that
options: only ministers can be held responsible for errors
a minister must hold his subordinates responsible for his failures
the prime minister can remove an erring minister
no individual can be held responsible for government decisions
questions.explanation: Individual ministerial responsibility means ministers are personally accountable to parliament for their department's actions, and the prime minister can dismiss an erring minister.

questions.question: The principle of collective responsibility does not operate under the presidential system of government because
options: the legislative and the executive work together
the executive is elected for a fixed term
the judiciary will overrule the impeachment of the cabinet
questions.explanation: Collective responsibility does not operate in presidential systems because the executive is elected for a fixed term and cannot be removed by a no-confidence vote from the legislature.
questions.hint: President serves fixed term regardless of legislature.

questions.question: Law making under military is done through
questions.explanation: Under military regimes, law making is done through the promulgation of decrees (federal) and edicts (state) by the military council, which serves as both executive and legislature.

questions.question: The most critical debates on a bill and amendments to it take place during the
questions.explanation: The second reading is the most critical stage where the bill's principles are debated, amendments are proposed, and members vote on whether the bill should proceed.
questions.hint: General debate on the bill's principles.

questions.question: The fundamental rights of citizens include
options: immunity from libel and right to employment
questions.explanation: Fundamental rights include the right to life and personal liberty, which are basic human rights protected by constitutions.
questions.hint: Basic rights like life and freedom.

questions.question: Electoral colleges have been criticized because they
options: create more seats in the legislature
sometimes negate the wishes of the electorate
questions.explanation: Electoral colleges are criticized because they can negate the popular vote, allowing a candidate who loses the popular vote to win the election (e.g., US presidential elections).
questions.hint: Popular vote winner may not win.

questions.question: Universal adult suffrage is usually restricted in most political systems to
options: those not more than 70 years old
questions.explanation: Universal adult suffrage is usually restricted to citizens, as voting rights are typically reserved for those who hold citizenship of a country.

questions.question: An election in which citizens are called upon to vote for or against a specified issue is called a
questions.explanation: A referendum is a direct vote in which citizens are asked to approve or reject a specific policy, law, or constitutional amendment.

questions.question: The most important objective of political parties is to
questions.explanation: The most important objective of political parties is to control government by winning elections and forming the government.

questions.question: The primary function of a pressure group is to
questions.explanation: The primary function of a pressure group is to influence government policy and decisions without seeking to form a government themselves.

questions.question: The biased presentation of information in order to influence public opinion is referred to as
questions.explanation: Propaganda is the biased or misleading presentation of information designed to influence public opinion, often using emotional appeals and selective facts.
questions.hint: Spreading biased information to shape views.

questions.question: Which of the following was a great military political power before the colonization of Nigeria
questions.explanation: The Igala Empire (Kingdom of Idah) was a powerful military and political force in pre-colonial Nigeria, controlling trade routes and exerting influence over neighboring peoples.
questions.hint: Kingdom of Idah was powerful in the lower Benue area.

questions.question: The Yoruba traditional governmental system was
questions.explanation: The Yoruba traditional governmental system was monarchical, with the Oba (king) as the political and spiritual head, supported by chiefs and council.

questions.question: During the pre-colonial period in Eastern Nigeria autocratic rule was made difficult by the
options: absence of a system of centralized authority
questions.explanation: In Eastern Nigeria, the absence of centralized authority (acephalous system) made autocratic rule difficult, as the Igbo political system was based on village assemblies and age grades.
questions.hint: No single king or central ruler.

questions.question: Vassalage as a political system was practised by the
questions.explanation: Vassalage (a feudal system where subordinates owe allegiance to a superior) was practiced by the Hausa-Fulani, with Emirs controlling subordinate chiefs and villages.
questions.hint: Feudal relationship between rulers and subjects.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a feature of colonial administration in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: Education levy was not a feature of colonial administration in Nigeria. Forced labor, low export prices, and various taxes (hut tax, poll tax) were common colonial features.
questions.hint: Colonialism involved exploitation, not education funding.

questions.question: Dual mandate was proposed by
questions.explanation: Lord Lugard proposed the Dual Mandate concept, arguing that colonial powers had a responsibility to develop their colonies for the benefit of both the colonizers and the colonized people.

questions.question: The Northern People's Congress stand during the nationalist struggle for Nigeria's independence was for
questions.explanation: The NPC favored gradual evolution towards independence, fearing that immediate independence would leave the North unprepared to compete politically with the more educated South.

questions.question: The Aba women riot was caused by the attempt to introduce
questions.explanation: The Aba Women's Riot (1929) was caused by the British attempt to introduce direct taxes on women in Eastern Nigeria, leading to massive protests.
questions.hint: Taxation of women sparked the protest.

questions.question: One of the unique innovations of the 1951 constitution was the introduction of
options: the office of the prime minister
questions.explanation: The Macpherson Constitution of 1951 introduced regional premiers (heads of government in each region) and regional executives, a significant step toward self-governance.

questions.question: The Constituent Assembly of 1978 had the primary responsibility of
options: drafting a constitution for the Second Republic
deliberating on, debating and amending the draft constitution
conducting a referendum on the draft constitution
questions.explanation: The 1978 Constituent Assembly was responsible for deliberating on, debating, and amending the draft constitution prepared by the Constitution Drafting Committee before it was promulgated.
questions.hint: They reviewed and amended the draft.

questions.question: To qualify to be President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria under the 1979 Constitution a candidate must
options: be at least 40 years of age
have the highest number of votes cast at the election
have not less than 1/4 of the votes cast at the election in each of at least 2/3 of all states in the federation
questions.explanation: Under the 1979 Constitution, a presidential candidate must win at least one-quarter (25%) of the votes in at least two-thirds (2/3) of the states, in addition to the highest total votes.

questions.question: By statute, the main responsibility of the armed forces of Nigeria is to
options: defend the sovereignty of the country
take part in peace-keeping operations in troubled neighbourhood countries
questions.explanation: The primary constitutional responsibility of the armed forces is to defend Nigeria's sovereignty and territorial integrity against external aggression.

questions.question: The highest court in Nigeria before 1963 was the
options: Judicial Committee of the Privy Council
questions.explanation: Before the 1963 Republican Constitution, the highest court of appeal for Nigeria was the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in London.

questions.question: The highest decision making body in Nigeria under the Buhari regime was the
questions.explanation: Under General Buhari's regime (1983-1985), the highest decision-making body was the Armed Forces Ruling Council (AFRC), which superseded the Supreme Military Council.

questions.question: Under the 1979 Constitution, the function of the Civil Service Commission include the
options: appointment, promotion and discipline of civil servants
negotiating better conditions of service for civil servants
questions.explanation: The Civil Service Commission is responsible for the appointment, promotion, and discipline of civil servants, ensuring a professional and impartial public service.
questions.hint: Handles personnel matters for civil servants.

questions.question: The primary factor which led to the formation of political parties in colonial Nigeria was
options: introduction of the elective principle and representative institutions
increased wealth of the indigenous elites
granting of self government by the colonial administration
call by black people all over the world for a final assault on colonialism
questions.explanation: The introduction of the elective principle and representative institutions (starting with the Clifford Constitution of 1922) created the need for political parties to contest elections.

questions.question: Three of the principles used in revenue allocation in Nigeria are
options: national interest, cultural ethnocentrism and national unity
land mass, primary school enrolment and geographical location
questions.explanation: Revenue allocation in Nigeria has historically used principles including derivation (tax from source), equality (equal distribution among states), and even development (balancing regional disparities).

questions.question: The results of the 1973 census in Nigeria were rejected because
options: the figures were unacceptable to the U.N.
Nigerians were contented with the existing population figures
there were preparations for a fresh census
questions.explanation: The 1973 census results were rejected because they were highly controversial and widely believed to have been manipulated for political and revenue allocation purposes.

questions.question: One of the advantages of privatization is that it
options: relieves government of its liquidity problem
makes the poor to be richer
gives private individuals control over public corporations
questions.explanation: Privatization is believed to make public corporations more effective and efficient by introducing private sector management, competition, and profit incentives.

questions.question: The most important aspect of the 1976 Local Government Reforms was the
options: introduction of a uniform system of local government
introduction of party politics at the local levels
subordination of local government to the Federal and state Governments
recognition of local government as autonomous and sovereign government
questions.explanation: The most important aspect of the 1976 reforms was the introduction of a uniform system of local government across Nigeria, with standardized structures, functions, and funding.

questions.question: Local governments were created in Nigeria mainly to
options: realise the objective of full employment
bring government nearer to the people
prevent the creation of more states
encourage competition and rivalry amongst ethnic groups
questions.explanation: Local governments are created to bring government closer to the people, ensuring grassroots participation and effective delivery of services at the local level.

questions.question: The Nigeria Military Government launched the Second National Development plan in 1970 purposely
options: to develop all the disadvantaged states
to accelerate the development of the country
for rapid economic and social development of the country after the civil war
to further the cause of a united, strong and self reliant nation
questions.explanation: The Second National Development Plan (1970-1974) was launched specifically for the rapid economic and social reconstruction of Nigeria after the devastating civil war (1967-1970).

questions.question: A feature unique to General Murtala Muhammed's Supreme Military Council as compared to that of General Yakubu Gowon was the
options: exclusion of military governors from the council
inclusion of the Chief Justice as a member
the exclusion of the Inspector-General of Police from the council
questions.explanation: A unique feature of Murtala Muhammed's Supreme Military Council was the inclusion of civilians as members, reflecting a broader consultation approach than Gowon's council.
questions.hint: Civilians were part of the council.

questions.question: A major problem of military governments in Nigeria is the
options: abolition of the post of ombudsman
absence of the rule of law
questions.explanation: A major problem of military governments is the absence of proper accountability, as they are not subject to the same democratic oversight and checks as civilian governments.

questions.question: In 1973, following an O.A.U. resolution, Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with
questions.explanation: In 1973, following an OAU resolution, Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with Israel in solidarity with Arab states and to protest Israeli occupation of Arab territories.

questions.question: Nigeria's foreign policy of non-alignment was a reaction to
questions.explanation: Nigeria's non-alignment policy was a reaction to the Cold War ideological competition between the Eastern (Soviet) and Western (US) blocs, choosing not to align with either.

questions.question: Which of the following countries had a strained relationship with Nigeria over the Angolan crisis of 1975?
questions.explanation: The United States had a strained relationship with Nigeria over the Angolan crisis (1975) because the US supported UNITA and FNLA factions, while Nigeria supported the MPLA.
questions.hint: US supported different faction in Angola.

questions.question: In the First Republic, Nigeria was very reluctant to have meaningful interaction with
questions.explanation: In the First Republic, Nigeria was reluctant to have meaningful interaction with the Soviet Union due to Cold War tensions and fears of communist influence.

questions.question: The major liberation organisation which fought for Namibia's independence was
questions.explanation: SWAPO (South West Africa People's Organization) was the major liberation movement that fought for Namibia's independence from South African rule, achieved in 1990.

questions.question: Which of these international organisations is the predecessor of the United Nations?
questions.explanation: The League of Nations (1919-1946) was the predecessor of the United Nations, established after WWI but failed to prevent WWII, leading to the UN's creation in 1945.

questions.question: The dramatic rise in the membership of the U.N. during late 1991 and early 1992 is best accounted for by the
options: admission of a large number of newly independent African States
appointment of the first African as Secretary General
break up of the Soviet Union into independent Republics
expansion of the membership of the Security Council
questions.explanation: The dramatic rise in UN membership in 1991-1992 was caused by the breakup of the Soviet Union into 15 independent republics, all of which joined the UN.

questions.question: Political authority refers to the
options: capacity to produce desired political results
capacity to reshape the political behaviour of citizens
recognized right to exercise political power
questions.explanation: Political authority is the recognized and legitimate right to exercise political power, based on consent, law, or tradition rather than mere force.

questions.question: The sovereignty of a state is determined by
questions.explanation: Sovereignty is determined by political aspects (supreme authority within territory) and legal aspects (recognition by other states and international law).

questions.question: The civil service belongs to the organ of government called the
questions.explanation: The civil service belongs to the executive branch of government, as it implements policies and administers programs under the direction of the executive.

questions.question: Ultimate power in a confederal state is
options: constitutionally divided between the centre and the units
questions.explanation: In a confederal system, ultimate power is vested in the constituent units (states or regions), which retain sovereignty and delegate limited powers to the central authority.

questions.question: Federalism is a constitutional arrangement in which
options: the national and regional governments derive their powers from the constitution
the national government acts only through the regional governments
regional and local authorities exist at the pleasure of the national government
the national government does not reach individual citizens directly
questions.explanation: Federalism is a constitutional arrangement where both national and regional governments derive their powers directly from the constitution, with each sovereign in its assigned sphere.
questions.hint: Both levels get power from the constitution.

questions.question: A written constitution is one that
options: can only be amended by the highest legislative body
is contained in a single document
questions.explanation: A written constitution is one where the fundamental laws and principles are codified in a single document or a set of documents.

questions.question: A federal system of government can exist without
questions.explanation: A federal system can exist without an executive presidential system; federalism is compatible with parliamentary systems (e.g., Canada, Australia, India, Germany).
questions.hint: Federalism works with parliamentary systems too.

questions.question: As a political ideology, communism seeks to
options: concentrate national wealth in the hands of a few
provide adequate opportunities for citizens to make profits
eliminate all forms of socio economic inequality
questions.explanation: Communism seeks to eliminate all forms of socioeconomic inequality through collective ownership of the means of production and a classless society.

questions.question: Nazism as a doctrine was proposed by
questions.explanation: Nazism (National Socialism) was proposed and led by Adolf Hitler in Germany, characterized by extreme nationalism, racism (Aryan supremacy), and dictatorship.

questions.question: Despite the doctrine of separation of powers the legislature plays quasi-judicial role as a result of its
questions.explanation: The legislature plays a quasi-judicial role through its investigative powers, conducting hearings, summoning witnesses, and performing oversight functions similar to courts.

questions.question: In the presidential system of government, the president is
options: elected only through the electoral college
Head of State and Head of Government
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, the president serves as both Head of State (ceremonial) and Head of Government (executive), fusing both roles in one office.
questions.hint: Same person is both ceremonial and executive leader.

questions.question: In the Westminster system of government, formal legislation can take the following forms EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Ministerial pronouncements are not formal legislation in the Westminster system. Formal legislation includes Acts of Parliament, Orders in Council, and Royal Proclamations.

questions.question: Delegated legislation is disadvantageous because it
options: erodes the principle of separation of powers
makes hasty review of legislation possible
increases the work of the legislature
questions.explanation: Delegated legislation erodes the principle of separation of powers by allowing the executive to make laws, which is traditionally a legislative function.

questions.question: The principle of the rule of law stipulates that
options: law makers are above the law
everybody is equal before the law
questions.explanation: The rule of law stipulates that everybody, including government officials and lawmakers, is equal before the law and subject to its provisions.
questions.hint: No one is above the law.

questions.question: Gerrymandering means
questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the manipulation of electoral district boundaries to favor a particular political party or group, often by creating oddly shaped constituencies.
questions.hint: Drawing districts to benefit a party.

questions.question: Another term for an electoral district is a
questions.explanation: An electoral district is commonly called a constituency - a territorial division from which a representative is elected to a legislative body.

questions.question: Franchise ensures
questions.explanation: Franchise (the right to vote) ensures citizen participation in public affairs, allowing people to choose their representatives and influence government decisions.

questions.question: Free and fair elections do NOT accommodate
options: obedience to the rules of the game
questions.explanation: Free and fair elections do not accommodate partial (biased) electoral administration, which undermines the integrity and credibility of the electoral process.

questions.question: Proportional representation is often criticized because it
options: tends to preserve the multi-party system
ensures that each party has a minister
provides that each state is represented according to its population
questions.explanation: Proportional representation is criticized because it tends to preserve the multi-party system, leading to coalition governments that can be unstable and weak.
questions.hint: Many small parties can lead to instability.

questions.question: Interest groups are important to democracies because they
options: represent groups who otherwise would be left out
provide a forum for confronting the government
questions.explanation: Interest groups are important because they represent specific groups (workers, businesses, professionals) that might otherwise be left out of the political process.
questions.hint: They give voice to specific interests.

questions.question: In the Habe political system, the Sarki was assisted in performing his executive functions by the
questions.explanation: In the Hausa political system (Habe), the Sarki (king) was assisted by the Galadima, who served as a key official responsible for administrative and executive functions.
questions.hint: Key administrative official in Hausa system.

questions.question: In the Oyo empire, the Alaafin was
questions.explanation: In the Oyo Empire, the Alaafin was a constitutional monarch whose powers were checked by the Oyo Mesi (council of chiefs) and the Bashorun, preventing absolute rule.
questions.hint: His powers were limited by council.

questions.question: Which of these sets represents the disadvantages of colonialism?
options: Exposure to western education, cultural imperialism and external trade relations
Cultural imperialism, external manipulate and foreign control of domestic economy
External manipulation, civil service evolution and liberal democracy
Liberal democracy, foreign domination and cultural imperialism
questions.explanation: The disadvantages of colonialism include cultural imperialism (imposition of foreign culture), external manipulation (political interference), and foreign control of the domestic economy.

questions.question: Imperialism was adopted by Europe to
options: expand its economic and political base
develop the economies of the colonies
establish a democratic society similar to that of Europe
end ethnic rivalries in the colonies
questions.explanation: Imperialism was adopted by European powers primarily to expand their economic (raw materials, markets) and political (strategic advantage, prestige) bases.

questions.question: The Lagos Colony and the Southern Protectorate of Nigeria were joined in the year
questions.explanation: The Lagos Colony and the Southern Nigeria Protectorate were joined in 1906 to form the Colony and Protectorate of Southern Nigeria.

questions.question: Tubman Goldie was famous for the
options: merger of British companies into the United African Company
treaty of friendship and the race of Nikki
questions.explanation: Sir George Tubman Goldie was famous for merging British trading companies into the United African Company (later Royal Niger Company), which facilitated British control over Nigeria.

questions.question: Which of the following internal factors did NOT affect the development of nationalist movements in colonial Nigeria?
options: Denial of equal opportunities to Nigerian vis-a-vis their European counterparts
The establishment of political parties and newspaper
the influx of West Indian and American intellectuals
questions.explanation: The influx of West Indian and American intellectuals was not a significant internal factor affecting Nigerian nationalism; it was more relevant to other African colonies.
questions.hint: Not a major factor in Nigeria.

questions.question: One significant aspect of the Legislative Council of 1922 was that
options: it made laws only for the southern Provinces
the Colony of Lagos and the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria were merged
laws governing land ownership in the north were extended to the south
questions.explanation: The Legislative Council of 1922 merged the Colony of Lagos and the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria for legislative purposes, though Northern Nigeria remained separate.
questions.hint: Lagos and Southern Protectorate came together.

questions.question: The 1960 Independence Constitution could be described as written
questions.explanation: The 1960 Independence Constitution was monarchical (Queen Elizabeth II as head of state represented by Governor-General) and parliamentary (Westminster-style system).
questions.hint: Queen was still head of state.

questions.question: The governor-general of Nigeria at the time of political independence was
questions.explanation: Sir James Robertson was the last Governor-General of Nigeria at independence in 1960, serving until Nigeria became a republic in 1963.

questions.question: Before the collapse of Nigeria's first republic in 1966, the prime minister was both
options: the head of government and a law maker
the head of state and Commander-in-Chief of the armed forces
Commander-in-Chief of the armed forces and party leader
Head of State and party leader
questions.explanation: Before the 1966 collapse, the Prime Minister was head of government and also a member of parliament (law maker), as in the Westminster system.

questions.question: In Nigeria, the function of the Council of states is
questions.explanation: The Council of States (now National Council of States) serves an advisory function to the President on matters such as state creation, boundary adjustments, and national security.

questions.question: Under the military administration, supreme court decisions are subject to review by
questions.explanation: Under military administration, Supreme Court decisions are final and not subject to review by any other court of law, though the military regime could overturn them by decree.

questions.question: Public Commissions established under the 1979 constitution were expected to be
questions.explanation: Public Commissions (e.g., Federal Electoral Commission, Public Service Commission) established under the 1979 Constitution were expected to be independent of executive control.

questions.question: Which of the following was an ad hoc Revenue Allocation Commission?
questions.explanation: The Phillipson Commission (1946) was an ad hoc commission appointed to review and make recommendations on revenue allocation in Nigeria.

questions.question: The term federal character was popularised by the
questions.explanation: The term 'federal character' was popularized by the 1975 Constitution Drafting Committee (CDC), which incorporated it into the draft 1979 Constitution to promote national unity.

questions.question: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as
questions.explanation: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as parastatals - government-owned enterprises established to provide essential services.

questions.question: The local government is an example of
questions.explanation: Local government is an example of devolution of power, where powers and functions are transferred from central to local authorities by statute.
questions.hint: Power passed down to local level.

questions.question: The body responsible for exercising the functions of local governments in the 1976 reforms was the
options: committee of chairmen and supervisory councillors
questions.explanation: The 1976 reforms established the Local Government Council as the body responsible for exercising local government functions, headed by a Chairman.

questions.question: Diarchy refers to the
options: rule by the government and the opposition parties
mixture of parliamentary and presidential systems
rule of political and economics elites
rule by the military and civilians
questions.explanation: Diarchy (or dyarchy) refers to a system of government with dual rule, such as the Babangida administration's mixture of military and civilian rule (1986-1993).

questions.question: Nigeria's relations with other nations is determined by her
questions.explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy and relations with other nations are determined primarily by her national interest - the protection and advancement of Nigeria's security, economic, and political goals.

questions.question: The foreign policy of Nigeria is centred on Africa because of her
options: concern for and attention to African problems
commitment to end colonization in Africa
questions.explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy is centered on Africa due to her genuine concern for and attention to African problems, including decolonization, apartheid, and development.

questions.question: The major organization through which Nigeria pursues her economic, political and social interest in West Africa is
questions.explanation: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) is the major organization through which Nigeria pursues her economic, political, and social interests in West Africa.

questions.question: A state is admitted into the United Nations on the
options: recommendation of the secretary-general to the security council
concurrent vote of the Security Council and the General Assembly
enabling action of the General Assembly
sponsorship of any member of the organization
questions.explanation: A state is admitted into the UN by a concurrent vote of the Security Council (recommendation) and the General Assembly (two-thirds majority approval).
questions.hint: Both Security Council and General Assembly must approve.

questions.question: The highest policy making body of the O.A.U. is the
options: Assembly of Heads of States and Governments
questions.explanation: The Assembly of Heads of State and Government is the highest policy-making body of the OAU (now African Union), composed of all member state presidents.

questions.question: To which of these bodies did Nigeria belong before the formation of the O.A.U in 1963?
questions.explanation: Before the OAU was formed in 1963, Nigeria belonged to the Monrovia Group, a bloc of moderate African states that favored gradual decolonization and cooperation.

questions.question: Who was the first Nigerian to be appointed President of the International Court of Justice at the Hague?
questions.explanation: Justice Taslim Olawale Elias was the first Nigerian to serve as President of the International Court of Justice (ICJ) at The Hague, from 1976 to 1979.
questions.hint: Served as ICJ President in the 1970s.

questions.question: An agency of the United Nations which specialize in the welfare of children is the
questions.explanation: UNICEF (United Nations Children's Fund) is the UN agency specializing in the welfare of children, including health, nutrition, education, and protection.

questions.question: Financial contributions to the liberation movements in Southern Africa by African governments were channelled through the
questions.explanation: Financial contributions to Southern African liberation movements were channelled through the OAU (Organization of African Unity) Liberation Committee.

questions.question: Prior to 1991, veto power was exercised in the United Nations Security Council by
options: France, Germany, the Soviet Union, Italy and Japan
Japan, the United States, Italy, Germany and France
the soviet Union, People Republic of China, Great Britain, France and the United States
Italy, the United States, the Soviet Union, Germany and France
questions.explanation: Prior to 1991, veto power was exercised by the five permanent members (P5) of the UN Security Council: the Soviet Union, China, Great Britain, France, and the United States.

questions.question: A society that is politically organised under a government is called a
questions.explanation: A polity is a politically organized society under a government, encompassing the entire political community including citizens, institutions, and governance structures.

questions.question: Political power is naked force when it is exercised without
questions.explanation: Political power becomes naked force when exercised without governmental legitimacy, meaning it relies on coercion rather than the consent and acceptance of the governed.
questions.hint: Power without legitimacy is just force.

questions.question: The major difference between a state and a nation is that the latter presupposes a
questions.explanation: A nation presupposes a homogeneous population with shared culture, language, and identity, while a state can have a heterogeneous population within a defined territory.

questions.question: The principle of judicial independence makes sense only when judges
options: have freedom to try any case
can join any political party of their choice
questions.explanation: Judicial independence requires judges to have permanent tenure of office (security of tenure), protecting them from arbitrary removal and allowing them to decide cases without fear.

questions.question: A federal system of government has the following three advantages
options: economics of scale, uniform development and political unity
economics of scale, uniform development and strong army
strong army, greater security and economics of scale
economic co-operation, uniform development and greater security
questions.explanation: Federalism offers advantages including economies of scale (efficient resource use), uniform development (balanced growth across regions), and political unity (national integration).

questions.question: Constitutionalism means
options: electoral processes based on the constitution
questions.explanation: Constitutionalism means respect for and adherence to the constitution, ensuring that government operates within constitutional limits and the rule of law prevails.

questions.question: Switzerland is often cited as a classic example of a country with
questions.explanation: Switzerland has a federal constitution, where power is shared between the central government and the cantons (states), each with constitutionally defined powers.

questions.question: What is the major distinguishing characteristic between flexible and rigid constitution?
questions.explanation: The major distinguishing characteristic is the amendment procedure: flexible constitutions can be amended by ordinary legislative process, while rigid constitutions require special procedures.
questions.hint: How easy it is to change.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines democracy?
options: Government based on virtuous principles and laws
Government based on the spirit and letter of the constitution
Government based on the free consent of the governed
Government which recognizes and respects human rights as enshrined in the constitution
questions.explanation: Democracy is best defined as government based on the free consent of the governed, where political authority derives from the people through popular participation and elections.

questions.question: An economic system in which the major means of production are owned and controlled by a few individuals is called
questions.explanation: Capitalism is an economic system where the major means of production are owned and controlled by private individuals or corporations, not by the state or community.

questions.question: The essence of checks and balances is to
options: enhance the functioning of government and prevent arbitrary use of power
ensure that people have their own rights
make sure that power is not delegated to other bodies or individuals
prevent political parties from putting pressure on government
questions.explanation: Checks and balances enhance government functioning and prevent arbitrary use of power by allowing each branch to limit the powers of the others, ensuring accountability.

questions.question: The power of a head of state to dissolve the legislature and order a general election is usually associated with the
questions.explanation: The power to dissolve the legislature and call elections is associated with the parliamentary system, where the head of state (or governor-general) acts on the prime minister's advice.

questions.question: Accountability in public office means that officers shall
options: keep proper account of states finances
render good account of their activities
questions.explanation: Accountability in public office means officers shall render a good account of their activities, explaining their decisions, actions, and use of public resources to relevant authorities.

questions.question: The act of bringing a legislative session to an end only for business to continue later is called
questions.explanation: Adjournment temporarily ends a legislative session with the intention to resume business later (e.g., overnight or weekend break), unlike prorogation which ends a session permanently.

questions.question: Which of the following violates the principle of the rule of law?
options: Arbitrary increases of prices by traders
Criticism of government actions by the press
Arbitrariness of individuals in the society
questions.explanation: Arbitrariness of government policies violates the rule of law because it means government acts without legal basis, predictability, or accountability, contrary to the principle that no one is above the law.

questions.question: An electoral system which restricts voting rights to only male adults is termed
questions.explanation: Male suffrage refers to an electoral system where voting rights are restricted to only adult males, excluding women from the franchise.

questions.question: Which of the following best represents duties and obligations of government?
questions.explanation: The provision of public utilities (roads, electricity, water, education, healthcare) is a primary duty and obligation of government to its citizens.

questions.question: An electoral process in which candidates for elective offices are selected by party members is known as
questions.explanation: A primary election is a process where political party members select candidates to represent the party in the general election.

questions.question: The principle whereby a legislator's tenure is abruptly brought to an end by his constituency is known as
questions.explanation: Recall is the process by which voters can remove an elected official from office before their term ends through a special election, a form of direct democracy.
questions.hint: Voters can remove their representative early.

questions.question: One of the main functions of a political party is
questions.explanation: Interest aggregation is a key function of political parties, where they combine diverse interests and demands from society into coherent policy programs and platforms.

questions.question: Which of the following traditional Nigerian societies practised the system of checks and balances?
questions.explanation: The Igbo traditional political system practiced checks and balances through the village assembly (Oha na Eze), age grades, title societies, and various institutions that limited any single authority's power.

questions.question: In terms of administrative structure, which of the following sets of pre-colonial Nigeria political systems do NOT match?
questions.explanation: Benin (centralized monarchy) and Igbo (acephalous decentralized system) do not match in administrative structure, as they represent opposite ends of the political organization spectrum.
questions.hint: One has a king, the other has no central ruler.

questions.question: Nationalist activities were earlier in British than in French West Africa because the
options: French West Africans were not interested in having self-government
British colonialists were no longer interested in governing their acquired territories
French administrative policies did not allow for political agitation
nationalists in British West Africa were more united than their French counterparts
questions.explanation: French administrative policies (assimilation and centralization) did not allow for political agitation, suppressing nationalist activities, while British indirect rule permitted more political expression.

questions.question: In British West Africa, the elective principle was first introduced in
questions.explanation: The elective principle was first introduced in The Gambia in 1847, earlier than in other British West African colonies, allowing for elected African representation.

questions.question: A thorny issue during the 1957/58 Nigerian constitutional conferences was the
options: leadership of the first post-independence government
control of the Mid West by the Western Region
questions.explanation: The problem of ethnic minorities was a thorny issue at the 1957/58 constitutional conferences, leading to the appointment of the Willink Commission to address minority fears.

questions.question: In the 1963 Constitution, there was separation of powers between the
options: president and the commander-in-chief of the armed forces
head of state and the president
head of government and the head of state
questions.explanation: In the 1963 Republican Constitution, there was separation of powers between the head of government (Prime Minister) and the head of state (President), with the President as ceremonial figure.
questions.hint: PM and President held separate offices.

questions.question: A major feature which differentiated the 1963 and 1979 constitutions was that in the former,
options: ministers were appointed from the National Assembly
ministers were appointed from outside the National Assembly
the prime minister was elected by the whole country
the National Assembly comprised the senate and the House of Assembly
questions.explanation: Under the 1963 parliamentary constitution, ministers were appointed from the National Assembly (members of parliament), while the 1979 presidential constitution allowed ministers from outside the legislature.

questions.question: A court order compelling the executive or its agencies to produce an unlawfully detained person is called a writ of
questions.explanation: Habeas corpus (Latin for 'you shall have the body') is a court order that requires the government to produce a detained person and justify their detention, protecting against unlawful imprisonment.

questions.question: The primary function of the Armed Forces of Nigeria is to
options: promote and protect the security of the nation
protect the head of states and commander in-chief of the armed forces of the nation
protect the citizens against corrupt and oppressive politicians
project the country's image by participating in peace keeping operations
questions.explanation: The primary constitutional function of the armed forces is to promote and protect the security of the nation, defending Nigeria's sovereignty and territorial integrity.

questions.question: Which organ was enshrined in the 1979 constitution to protect public servants from political interference and arbitrary dismissal?
questions.explanation: The Federal Civil Service Commission was established under the 1979 Constitution to protect civil servants from political interference and arbitrary dismissal, handling appointments, promotions, and discipline.

questions.question: The political party that replaced NNDP before independence was
questions.explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) replaced the Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) as the dominant political force in Lagos politics in the late 1930s before independence.

questions.question: Apart from discussing the draft of the 1979 constitution, what was the major pre-occupation of the Constituent Assembly in 1978?
questions.explanation: Apart from the draft constitution, the Sharia issue (the establishment of Sharia Court of Appeal at the federal level) was a major pre-occupation of the 1978 Constituent Assembly.

questions.question: The first minority state to be created in the Nigerian federation was
questions.explanation: The Mid-West Region (now Edo and Delta states) was the first minority state created in Nigeria in 1963, carved out of the Western Region to address minority concerns.

questions.question: One major constraint on the smooth operation of federalism in Nigeria is the
options: lack of an acceptable revenue allocation formula
inability to conduct a successful census
questions.explanation: The lack of an acceptable revenue allocation formula has been a major constraint on Nigerian federalism, causing constant disputes between federal and state governments over resource sharing.

questions.question: One of the basic differences between ministries and public corporations is that while ministries are
options: run on rules, public corporations are not
wholly owned by the government, public corporations are owned by both government and individuals
not established with specific statutes, public corporations are
not important in developmental process, public corporations are
questions.explanation: Ministries are not established with specific statutes (they exist by administrative order), while public corporations are created by specific enabling statutes or Acts of Parliament.

questions.question: Privatization and commercialization of public enterprises in Nigeria will lead to further entrenchment of
questions.explanation: Privatization and commercialization shift ownership from government to private hands, further entrenching capitalism (private ownership of the means of production).

questions.question: The 1976 Local Government Reforms has
options: reduced political corruption at the local level
resulted in accelerated development in the rural areas
created more autonomy for local administration
reduced the political roles of traditional rulers
questions.explanation: The 1976 Local Government Reforms reduced the political roles of traditional rulers by giving them Chief-in-Council (advisory) status, removing their executive powers.

questions.question: The Babangida administration's transition to civil rule programme officially started in
questions.explanation: The Babangida administration's transition to civil rule programme officially started in 1987 with the establishment of the Political Bureau and the transition timetable.

questions.question: A major negative impact of military intervention in Nigerian politics is the
options: domination of the economy by foreign powers
poor performance of the military in foreign peace-keeping operations
neglect of the welfare of military personnel
questions.explanation: A major negative impact of military intervention is the politicization of the military, where soldiers become involved in politics, undermining professionalism and discipline.

questions.question: The committee that recommended Abuja as the new Federal Capital was headed by
questions.explanation: Justice Akinola Aguda headed the committee that recommended Abuja as Nigeria's new federal capital, replacing Lagos, in 1976.

questions.question: The term, comprador bourgeoisie, has been used by scholars to describe
options: Nigeria businessmen who represent foreign interests
questions.explanation: Comprador bourgeoisie refers to local businessmen who represent and serve foreign economic interests, acting as intermediaries between foreign capitalists and the domestic market.

questions.question: Which of the following countries is closely associated with the development of Ajaokuta Steel Project?
questions.explanation: Russia (formerly the Soviet Union) was closely associated with the development of the Ajaokuta Steel Project, providing technical assistance and funding.

questions.question: The foreign affairs minister who advocated the 'Concert of Medium Powers' was
questions.explanation: Professor Bolaji Akinyemi, as Nigeria's Foreign Minister (1985-1987), advocated the 'Concert of Medium Powers' as a strategy for Nigeria to exert influence in international affairs.

questions.question: Which of the following reasons best explains why Nigeria during the first republic, could not pursue an independent foreign policy?
options: The numerous differences among the ethnic groups
Nigeria's economy was dependent on the Western countries
questions.explanation: During the First Republic, Nigeria's economy was dependent on Western countries (especially Britain) for trade, investment, and technical assistance, limiting foreign policy independence.

questions.question: The British prime minister who opposed the cancellation of Nigeria's debt was
questions.explanation: British Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher opposed the cancellation of Nigeria's debt during the 1980s, insisting on repayment terms that Nigeria found harsh.
questions.hint: Iron Lady was tough on debt.

questions.question: Which of the following African countries received Nigeria's assistance to fight her war of independence in 1975/76?
questions.explanation: Angola received Nigeria's assistance in its war of independence (1975-1976), with Nigeria supporting the MPLA faction against Portuguese colonial rule and rival factions.

questions.question: Which Nigerian was the executive secretary of the ECA?
questions.explanation: Professor Adebayo Adedeji served as the Executive Secretary of the United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) from 1975 to 1991.

questions.question: Nigeria was regarded as a 'Frontline State' because she
options: assisted liberation struggles in Southern Africa
sent troops for peace-keeping in Somalia
questions.explanation: Nigeria was regarded as a Frontline State because it actively assisted liberation struggles in Southern Africa against apartheid and colonial rule, providing financial and military support.

questions.question: The major strategy used by OPEC to influence oil price is by
options: determining the quantity of oil to be produced at any given period
influencing buyers at the international market to buy at high price
allowing member countries to produce at their discretion
increasing the supply of the commodity
questions.explanation: OPEC influences oil prices primarily by determining production quotas for member countries, controlling the quantity of oil supplied to the global market.

questions.question: The Gulf war of 1990 is an indication that the U.N.O. is unable to
questions.explanation: The Gulf War of 1990 indicated that the UNO is unable to ensure permanent world peace, as it could not prevent Iraq's invasion of Kuwait despite diplomatic efforts.

questions.question: The concept of sovereignty can best be defined as the
options: state of being supreme in authority
questions.explanation: Sovereignty is the state of being supreme in authority, where a state has absolute and independent power over its territory and citizens, subject to no external authority.

questions.question: Power is understood to mean the
options: relationship between the government and the people
desire to seize the state apparatus
questions.explanation: Power is the capacity or ability to compel obedience, influence behavior, or achieve desired outcomes, even against resistance.
questions.hint: Ability to get others to do what you want.

questions.question: The institutions which constitute the main organs of the government of a nation are the
options: legislature, the executive and the judiciary
questions.explanation: The three main organs of government are the legislature (law-making), the executive (law-implementing), and the judiciary (law-interpreting).

questions.question: The executive arm of government includes the
questions.explanation: The executive arm includes the police, armed forces, civil service, and other agencies that implement and enforce laws, while courts belong to the judiciary and national assembly to the legislature.

questions.question: Unicameral legislature is often criticized for
options: condoning the participation of traditional rulers
questions.explanation: Unicameral legislature is criticized for restricting the scope of participation, as there is no second chamber to provide additional review, representation, or checks on legislation.

questions.question: A legal document yet to be enacted into law but still under debate in the legislature is known as
questions.explanation: A bill is a proposed law under consideration by the legislature. It becomes an Act (law) only after being passed by the legislature and assented to by the head of state.

questions.question: According to Marxist theory, those who own and control the means of production in a capitalist society are the
questions.explanation: In Marxist theory, the bourgeoisie are the capitalist class who own and control the means of production (factories, land, resources) and exploit the proletariat (working class).

questions.question: A distinctive feature of a democratic system of government is the
options: existence of a single political party
centralization of all power in the executive
questions.explanation: A distinctive feature of democracy is the existence of periodic, free, and competitive elections, allowing citizens to choose and hold leaders accountable.

questions.question: The goal of the theory of separation of power is to
options: make the executive dependent on the legislature
give more powers to the legislature
questions.explanation: The goal of separation of powers is to protect individual liberty by preventing the concentration of power in any single branch, thereby avoiding tyranny.

questions.question: The power of the courts to declare legislative enactments as unconstitutional is known as judicial
questions.explanation: Judicial review is the power of courts to examine legislative acts and executive actions and declare them unconstitutional if they violate the constitution.
questions.hint: Courts check if laws are constitutional.

questions.question: Bye-laws made by the Minister of Transport for running the Nigerian Railway Corporation is an exercise of
questions.explanation: Bye-laws made by a minister represent delegated legislation, where the legislature delegates law-making authority to executive agencies or officials to make detailed rules.
questions.hint: Power given by parliament to minister.

questions.question: The application of the rule of law can be limited by
options: securing the tenure of office of judges
questions.explanation: The application of the rule of law can be limited by invoking emergency powers, which may suspend certain rights and allow executive actions that bypass normal legal procedures.
questions.hint: State of emergency can limit rights.

questions.question: Laws made by the attorney-general and minister of justice are called
questions.explanation: Laws made by the attorney-general and minister of justice are called ex post facto laws, which retroactively criminalize actions that were legal when performed (generally prohibited).

questions.question: The legal process by which an alien is accepted as a national of a country other than his own is known as
questions.explanation: Naturalization is the legal process by which a foreign national (alien) becomes a citizen of another country, typically after meeting residency and other requirements.

questions.question: The inalienable rights of the citizen are best protected through
questions.explanation: The inalienable rights of citizens are best protected through a constitution that enshrines fundamental rights, provides legal guarantees, and limits government power.

questions.question: Enfranchisement describes the process of
options: allowing eligible persons the right to vote
allowing special categories of persons to contest elections
allowing all women the right to vote
questions.explanation: Enfranchisement is the process of granting the right to vote to eligible persons who were previously disenfranchised (excluded from voting).

questions.question: An electoral system that apportions legislative seats to a group relative to its actual voting strength is referred to as
questions.explanation: Proportional representation apportions legislative seats to parties in proportion to their share of the popular vote, ensuring representation matches voting strength.

questions.question: A non party election occurs when
questions.explanation: A non-party election occurs when all candidates run as independents without political party affiliation, as in local government elections in some systems.

questions.question: The major distinguishing factor between a political party and a pressure group is that while the former
options: is effectively organized, the latter is not
engages in propaganda, the latter is not
seeks power, the latter influences it
does not form a government, the latter does
questions.explanation: Political parties seek to control government power by winning elections and forming government, while pressure groups seek to influence government policy without seeking power themselves.
questions.hint: Parties want to govern, groups want to influence.

questions.question: Public opinion is the view expressed by a
options: few articulate members in the society
majority of the members of a society
group of individuals in the society
questions.explanation: Public opinion is the view expressed by a significant majority of members of a society on political issues, representing the collective sentiment of the population.

questions.question: On what is charismatic authority based?
questions.explanation: Charismatic authority is based on the personal ability, exceptional qualities, influence, and magnetic personality of an individual leader, as described by Max Weber.

questions.question: In which of the following Nigerian pre-colonial political units was the principle of separation of power institutionalized?
questions.explanation: The Oyo Empire institutionalized separation of powers with the Alaafin (king), Oyo Mesi (council of chiefs), and Bashorun (prime minister) serving as checks on each other's power.

questions.question: The geographical entity in which the traditional ruler was both the religious and political head was the
questions.explanation: In the Sokoto Caliphate, the Sultan (and Emirs) served as both religious (Islamic) and political heads, making it a theocratic system.

questions.question: In which order did the various European groups penetrate into the interior of present-day Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The order of European penetration into Nigeria was: Explorers (mapping the territory), Traders (commerce), Missionaries (religious conversion), and Imperialists (political control/colonization).

questions.question: Indirect rule was introduced in Nigeria because
options: there were few administrators in the country
the native authorities were already well organized
questions.explanation: Indirect rule was introduced due to manpower shortages - Britain had too few colonial administrators to govern directly, so they ruled through traditional rulers.

questions.question: The breakdown of the Macpherson Constitution was partly caused by the crisis within the
questions.explanation: The breakdown of the Macpherson Constitution (1951) was partly caused by the crisis within the Action Group (AG), particularly the leadership conflict and the 1953 motion crisis.

questions.question: The first leader of the National council of Nigeria and the Cameroons was
questions.explanation: Herbert Macaulay was the first president of the National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC), founded in 1944, with Nnamdi Azikiwe as secretary.

questions.question: Under the 1963 Constitution, a bill could only become law if it was accented to by the
questions.explanation: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, a bill became law after being accented to by the President (ceremonial head of state), who replaced the British monarch.

questions.question: The first executive president of Nigeria was
questions.explanation: Alhaji Shehu Shagari was the first executive president of Nigeria (1979-1983) under the 1979 Constitution, which established the presidential system.

questions.question: Which of the following organs control the Civil service?
questions.explanation: The civil service is controlled by parliament (through legislation, oversight, and appropriations) and the courts (through judicial review of administrative actions).

questions.question: Under the Civil Service Reforms of 1988. the Nigerian Civil Service became
questions.explanation: The 1988 Civil Service Reforms (under Babangida) professionalized the civil service by introducing the Director-General cadre and separating technical from political roles.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a function of FEDECO during the second republic?
options: Organizing, conducting and supervising all elections
Providing guidelines, rules and regulations for political parties
Arranging for the registration of persons qualified to vote
questions.explanation: Swearing-in of elected candidates was not a function of FEDECO (Federal Electoral Commission); that was done by the judiciary or legislative officials.
questions.hint: FEDECO ran elections, didn't swear in winners.

questions.question: Who among the following served as chairmen of NEC under General Babangida?
questions.explanation: Professor Eme Awa and Professor Humphrey Nwosu served as chairmen of the National Electoral Commission (NEC) under General Babangida's transition programme.

questions.question: The Minorities Commission appointed in 1957 in Nigeria was headed by
questions.explanation: The Minorities Commission (Willink Commission) was headed by Sir Henry Willink, appointed in 1957 to investigate minority fears and make recommendations on state creation.

questions.question: Nigeria adopted the nineteen-state structure in
questions.explanation: Nigeria adopted the nineteen-state structure in 1976 under the Murtala/Obasanjo administration, increasing from 12 states created in 1967.

questions.question: Privatization of public corporations in Nigeria implies
options: the public take over of companies
questions.explanation: Privatization implies government divestment from public corporations, transferring ownership and control to private entities.

questions.question: Examples of public corporations in Nigeria are
questions.explanation: NEPA (National Electric Power Authority, now PHCN) and NRC (Nigerian Railway Corporation) are public corporations, while UAC and CFAO are private companies.

questions.question: Presidentialism in Local government administration is understood to mean
options: the abolition of the role of traditional rulers as chairmen
local government chief executives to be known as presidents
chairmen of local governments to serve as accounting officers
the phasing out of the post of the concilors
questions.explanation: Presidentialism in local government means that the chairman serves as the chief executive and accounting officer, responsible for financial management and administration.

questions.question: Local government have been stripped of one of their important functions through the establishment of the
questions.explanation: The National Primary Education Commission (NPEC) took over primary education management from local governments, stripping them of this important function.

questions.question: The military administration that attempted to tackle the problem of inefficiency in the public sector by mass retirement of public officers was the
questions.explanation: The Buhari administration (1983-1985) attempted to tackle inefficiency through mass retirement of public officers, including a purge of the civil service.

questions.question: Under military regimes in Nigeria, the branches of government that were fused are the
options: executive, the legislature and the judiciary
questions.explanation: Under military regimes, the executive and legislature are fused, as the military council performs both executive and legislative functions by issuing decrees.
questions.hint: Military council makes and executes laws.

questions.question: Nigeria is the major shareholder in the
questions.explanation: Nigeria is a major shareholder in the African Development Bank (AfDB), reflecting its significant economic role and contributions to African development.

questions.question: Nigerian soldiers have been deployed for peace keeping mission to
questions.explanation: Nigerian soldiers have been deployed for peacekeeping missions to Somalia (UNOSOM) and Rwanda (UNAMIR) during the 1990s crises in those countries.

questions.question: The adoption of non-alignment as a principle of Nigerian foreign policy is aimed at
options: promoting Nigerian's leadership aspiration in Africa
placing Nigeria on a comfortable position to contribute to international peace and morality
enabling Nigeria assume equal status with the world powers
fulfilling a basic requirement for her acceptance into the Security Council of the UN
questions.explanation: Non-alignment aims to place Nigeria in a comfortable position to contribute to international peace and morality, avoiding alignment with Cold War blocs while promoting African interests.

questions.question: The country whose interest conflicted with those of Nigeria over Angola in 1975 was
questions.explanation: US interests conflicted with Nigeria over Angola in 1975, as the US supported UNITA/FNLA factions while Nigeria supported the MPLA.

questions.question: The principle of Africa being the centre-piece of Nigeria's foreign policy has been demonstrated in
questions.explanation: Nigeria's Africa-centred foreign policy was demonstrated in Namibia, where Nigeria supported SWAPO's liberation struggle and contributed to Namibian independence.

questions.question: Which of the following countries has the right to veto the decision of the Security Council of the UNO?
questions.explanation: China is one of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council with veto power. Switzerland, Argentina, and Sweden are not permanent members.

questions.question: The primary objective of the UNO is to
options: serve as a co-ordinating platform for the actions of nations
questions.explanation: The primary objective of the United Nations is to maintain international peace and security, preventing future wars and promoting peaceful resolution of disputes.

questions.question: The OAU is founded on the principle of
options: settlement of international disputes by armed struggles
justifiable interference in the domestic affairs of member states
questions.explanation: The OAU (now AU) was founded on the principle of sovereign equality of member states, respecting the territorial integrity and independence of each member.

questions.question: One of the achievements of ECOWAS is the
questions.explanation: One achievement of ECOWAS is the adoption of the Brown Card (vehicle insurance scheme) that allows vehicles to travel across West Africa with a single insurance policy.

questions.question: Authority refers to the
options: might to secure compliance from others within a given social setting
power to exercise might over others behaviour
mandate to exercise power over others
ability to compel others to act in a particular way
questions.explanation: Authority is the legitimate or legally established right (mandate) to exercise power over others, based on consent, law, or tradition rather than mere force.

questions.question: Government is different from other political organizations because
options: it has legitimate power over citizens
its officials have fixed terms of office
it can punish those who violate its rules
it is made up of elected officials
questions.explanation: Government is distinct because it has legitimate power (authority) over citizens, backed by the state's monopoly on the legitimate use of force within a territory.

questions.question: Judicial independence in a modern democracy can be ensured by
options: the confirmation of the appointment of judges by the legislature
the provision that judges can only be removed from office by the president-in-council
making judges independent of the ministry of justice
safeguarding the security of tenure of judges
questions.explanation: Judicial independence is ensured by safeguarding the security of tenure of judges, protecting them from arbitrary removal and allowing them to decide cases without fear.

questions.question: The official report of proceedings in parliament is known as the
questions.explanation: Hansard is the official verbatim report of debates and proceedings in parliament, named after Thomas Curson Hansard who first published it in Britain.

questions.question: An important feature of the federal system of government is the existence of a
questions.explanation: A key feature of federalism is the existence of multiple tiers of government (federal, state, and local), each with constitutionally defined powers and responsibilities.

questions.question: An important aspect of an unwritten constitution is that it
options: is easy to understand by everybody
questions.explanation: An unwritten constitution (like Britain's) contains customary laws, conventions, traditions, and judicial precedents rather than being codified in a single document.

questions.question: The ideology that advocates the complete control of the sources of power is
questions.explanation: Totalitarianism advocates complete government control over all sources of power, including economic, political, social, and cultural aspects of society.

questions.question: Shadow cabinet is associated with the
questions.explanation: The shadow cabinet is associated with the parliamentary system, where the opposition party appoints spokespersons who shadow each government minister.

questions.question: One basic characteristic of parliamentary system of government is that the
options: upper and lower houses of the legislature have equal powers
cabinet is part of the legislature
prime minister is usually a member of the upper house
opposition party members are also included in the cabinet
questions.explanation: In a parliamentary system, the cabinet (executive) is drawn from and part of the legislature, with ministers also being members of parliament.

questions.question: In a presidential system, the theory of separation of power is not absolute because the president
options: can declare a state of emergency
can be impeached by the court
can declare laws null and void
questions.explanation: The separation of powers is not absolute because the president assents to bills (legislative function), representing executive participation in the legislative process.

questions.question: The principle of checks and balance is found mainly in
questions.explanation: Checks and balances are mainly found in the presidential system, where separate branches of government have powers to limit each other's actions.

questions.question: During the second republic in Nigeria, the branch of the legislature responsible for ratifying presidential nominees for appointments was the
questions.explanation: During the Second Republic (1979-1983), the Senate was responsible for ratifying presidential nominees for appointments to key positions.

questions.question: A tax law originates from
questions.explanation: A tax law originates from a public bill (also called a money bill), which deals with matters affecting the general population such as taxation and government revenue.

questions.question: The order used by the court against unnecessary detention is
questions.explanation: Habeas corpus is the court order used against unlawful or unnecessary detention, requiring the detaining authority to produce the person and justify their detention.

questions.question: When school pupils sing the national anthem and salute the flag, they are
options: practicing to be leaders of tomorrow
carrying out their responsibilities as citizens
questions.explanation: Singing the national anthem and saluting the flag are obligations (duties) of citizens, demonstrating loyalty, patriotism, and respect for national symbols.

questions.question: A major duty of citizen is to
questions.explanation: A major duty of citizens is to pay taxes, which provides revenue for government to provide public services and infrastructure.

questions.question: The simple plurality electoral system is often criticized because
options: the ruling party may lose the election
the winner may not poll an absolute majority
it works against all opposition parties
