questions.explanation: The simple plurality system is criticized because the winner may not poll an absolute majority (over 50%), winning with less than half the votes cast.

questions.question: The process of dividing a country into electoral districts is known as
questions.explanation: Delimitation is the process of dividing a country into electoral districts or constituencies for election purposes, often based on population distribution.

questions.question: Political parties are formed essentially to
options: compete with the military for power
questions.explanation: Political parties are formed essentially to capture state power by winning elections and forming the government.

questions.question: A systematic effort to manipulate the beliefs, attitudes and actions of the public through the mass media and other means is called
questions.explanation: Propaganda is the systematic effort to manipulate public beliefs, attitudes, and actions through biased or misleading information spread via mass media and other channels.

questions.question: In the Igbo political system, authority was shared among
options: men and women with ozo titles
all age groups and warrant chiefs
elders of the community and ofo title holders
questions.explanation: In the Igbo political system, authority was shared among elders of the community (village heads) and holders of the ofo title, which symbolized ancestral authority and justice.

questions.question: The crucial check on an autocratic Oba in the pre-colonial Yoruba political system was
options: presenting to him a suicide symbol
refusing to carry out his orders
questions.explanation: The crucial check on an autocratic Oba was presenting to him a suicide symbol (parrot eggs or empty calabash), which signaled the Oba to commit suicide to avoid shame and preserve the kingdom.
questions.hint: Symbolic request for the Oba to die.

questions.question: A major feature of the system of government in the Sokoto Caliphate was that
options: it was based on customs and traditions
questions.explanation: The Sokoto Caliphate was theocratic, with the Sultan and Emirs serving as both religious (Islamic) and political heads of their domains.

questions.question: The people of Southern Nigeria first came in contact with Europeans through
questions.explanation: The people of Southern Nigeria first came in contact with Europeans through peaceful trade, initially in goods like palm oil, ivory, and later slaves.

questions.question: Nigeria's first law making body after the amalgamation was known as the
questions.explanation: After the 1914 amalgamation, Nigeria's first law-making body was the Legislative Council, established by the 1914 Nigerian Council (advisory) and later replaced by the 1922 Clifford Constitution's Legislative Council.

questions.question: One major flaw of the British indirect rule in Nigeria was that the system
options: encouraged the use of European administrators
questions.explanation: Indirect rule championed ethnocentrism by imposing Hausa-Fulani administrative structures on other ethnic groups (especially in Eastern Nigeria) where they were not suitable.
questions.hint: Imposed one culture's system on others.

questions.question: The nationalist organization formed in Nigeria in the late 1930s was the
options: National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons
questions.explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) was formed in the late 1930s (1934), and became a major nationalist organization in Nigeria before independence.

questions.question: In 1947, Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe led a delegation to the British Colonial Office in London to protest against the
options: appointment of Bernard Bourdillon as Governor-General
appointment of Oliver Lyttelton as colonial secretary
questions.explanation: In 1947, Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe led an NCNC delegation to London to protest against the provisions of the Richards Constitution (1946), which they felt was imposed without Nigerian consultation.

questions.question: Under the 1922 Clifford constitution, franchise was granted in Calabar and Lagos to
options: persons with an annual income of at least 100
male adults with an annual income of at least 100
questions.explanation: Under the Clifford Constitution of 1922, the franchise was granted to male adults with an annual income of at least £100, a very restrictive property qualification.

questions.question: Under the 1979 Constitution in Nigeria, each state of the federation
options: had equal legislators in the House of Representatives
had an equal number of senators
was provided with a presidential liaison officer
questions.explanation: Under the 1979 Constitution, each state had an equal number of senators (5 senators per state), ensuring equal state representation in the Senate.

questions.question: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the power of judicial review was vested in the
options: the chief justice of the federation
questions.explanation: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the power of judicial review (declaring laws unconstitutional) was vested in the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Between 1966 and 1975, the highest legislative body in Nigeria was the
questions.explanation: Between 1966 and 1975 (Gowon era), the highest legislative body was the Supreme Military Council (SMC), which served as both executive and legislature.

questions.question: Three important concepts associated with a well organized civil service are
questions.explanation: Three important concepts of a well-organized civil service are neutrality (political impartiality), anonymity (not publicly identified with policies), and impartiality (fairness to all).

questions.question: The public agency now mandated to register births and deaths in Nigeria is the
questions.explanation: The National Population Commission (NPC) is mandated to register births and deaths in Nigeria, in addition to conducting censuses.

questions.question: Under the Babangida administration, the political bureau recommended at the federal level,
questions.explanation: The Political Bureau (1986) recommended a bicameral legislature (Senate and House of Representatives) and a two-party system (SDP and NRC) for the Third Republic.

questions.question: The first political party that contested election in Nigeria after the Clifford Constitution was
options: National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons
questions.explanation: The Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP), founded by Herbert Macaulay in 1923, was the first political party to contest elections under the Clifford Constitution.

questions.question: Pre-independence political parties in Nigeria included the
questions.explanation: Pre-independence political parties included NNDP (1923), NCNC (1944), NPC (1951), and AG (1951) as major parties before independence in 1960.

questions.question: In Nigeria, functions shared by the central and state governments include
options: external representation, education and provision of water
construction of roads, defence and health
control of ports, health and education
education, provision of water and construction of roads
questions.explanation: Education, provision of water, and construction of roads are concurrent powers shared by both federal and state governments in Nigeria's federal system.

questions.question: The creation of more states in Nigeria has
options: reduced the gap between rich and poor states
increased the powers of the federal government
increased the power of the states and local governments
questions.explanation: The creation of more states has increased the powers of the federal government by making it more central to revenue allocation and inter-governmental relations.

questions.question: After the botched coup of January 1966, power was handed over to General Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi by the
questions.explanation: After the January 15, 1966 coup, power was handed over to General Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi by the Council of Ministers (the federal cabinet), as he was the most senior military officer.

questions.question: Nigeria's major export commodities in the early years of her independence were
questions.explanation: In the early years of independence, Nigeria's major export commodities were agricultural products: cocoa (West), palm oil (East), and groundnuts (North).

questions.question: The foreign affairs minister who introduced economic diplomacy as a foreign policy initiative was
questions.explanation: Major-General Ike Nwachukwu introduced economic diplomacy as a foreign policy initiative during his tenure as Foreign Minister under the Babangida administration.

questions.question: Non-alignment is no longer relevant in Nigeria foreign policy because
options: she is seen as the leader of Africa
her focus is now on Africa
of the formation of the ECOWAS
questions.explanation: Non-alignment is no longer relevant because the Cold War (the East-West ideological competition) has ended, making alignment with superpowers less significant.

questions.question: In 1978, the Obasanjo Administration nationalized the assets of the British Petroleum and the Barclays Bank in Nigeria in reaction to the British
options: continued trade links with South Africa
reluctance to write off Nigeria's debts
questions.explanation: In 1978, Nigeria nationalized BP and Barclays assets because Britain continued trade links with apartheid South Africa, defying international sanctions.

questions.question: Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with France in 1961 because of
options: France's atomic test in the Sahara Desert
the poor relations of the Franco-Phone countries with her
General de-Gaulle's negative attitude towards her
questions.explanation: Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with France in 1961 because France conducted atomic tests in the Sahara Desert (Algeria), which threatened African security and environment.

questions.question: In 1993, Nigeria troops were on peace-keeping assignment to
questions.explanation: In 1993, Nigerian troops were deployed on peace-keeping missions to Liberia (ECOMOG) and Somalia (UNOSOM).

questions.question: Nigeria established a trust fund for other African countries with the
options: International Bank for Reconstruction and Development
questions.explanation: Nigeria established a trust fund for other African countries with the African Development Bank (AfDB) to support development projects across the continent.

questions.question: Nations join international organizations so that they could
questions.explanation: Nations join international organizations primarily to advance their national interests, including security, economic benefits, and diplomatic influence.

questions.question: The organ of the U.N.O. with full representation is the
questions.explanation: The General Assembly has full representation, with all member states having equal representation (one vote each), regardless of size or power.

questions.question: Which of the following was the last to win independence from colonial rule?
questions.explanation: Angola was the last to win independence among these, gaining independence from Portugal on November 11, 1975, after a prolonged liberation struggle.

questions.question: Which of the following defines the concept of government?
options: The process of administering justice in a country
The process of supervising the activities of legislature
The orderly management and control of the affairs of a country
The orderly transfer of power to duly elected politicians
questions.explanation: Government is the orderly management and control of the affairs of a country, including making and enforcing laws, and administering public policies.

questions.question: The most distinguishing characteristic of the state is
questions.explanation: Sovereignty is the most distinguishing characteristic of the state, representing its supreme and independent authority over its territory and citizens.

questions.question: The judiciary assigns clear meaning to the laws by
questions.explanation: The judiciary assigns clear meaning to laws through interpretation, resolving ambiguities and applying laws to specific cases.

questions.question: The structure of government implies the
options: organization of power and functions of government
method of revenue allocation by government
questions.explanation: The structure of government refers to the organization of power and functions among the three branches: legislature, executive, and judiciary.

questions.question: If the central government has less power than the component units, the constitution is said to be
questions.explanation: If the central government has less power than component units (states), the constitution is confederal, where states retain sovereignty and the centre is weak.

questions.question: In democracy, ultimate authority resides in the
questions.explanation: In democracy, ultimate authority resides in the people (popular sovereignty), who exercise it through voting and participation in governance.
questions.hint: People are the source of power.

questions.question: A major characteristic of the socialist system is its provision for
questions.explanation: A major characteristic of socialism is public ownership (collective or state ownership) of the means of production, rather than private ownership.

questions.question: In the presidential system of government, the chief executive is
options: selected by the party with the majority of seats in the legislature
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, the chief executive (president) is elected by the entire electorate (direct popular vote or electoral college), not by the legislature.

questions.question: The resignation of the cabinet after a defeat in parliament is an expression of the principle of
questions.explanation: Cabinet resignation after a parliamentary defeat expresses the principle of collective responsibility, where the entire government takes responsibility and resigns together.

questions.question: Ceremonial and executive powers are fused in the
questions.explanation: In the presidential system, ceremonial (Head of State) and executive (Head of Government) powers are fused in the same person - the President.

questions.question: The empowerment of the Police Service commission to make rules for the appointment, promotion and dismissal of its personnel, is an example of
questions.explanation: This is an example of delegated legislation, where parliament delegates law-making authority to administrative bodies like the Police Service Commission to make detailed rules.
questions.hint: Parliament gives power to make rules.

questions.question: The principle by which both the governors and the governed are bound by the law is called
questions.explanation: Constitutionalism is the principle that both governors (government officials) and the governed (citizens) are bound by the law, ensuring limited government.

questions.question: The rights of a citizen can be withdrawn by the state if the person
questions.explanation: Citizens' rights can be withdrawn or restricted if the person is convicted by a court (e.g., imprisonment may suspend certain rights like voting or freedom of movement).

questions.question: In a democracy, franchise is given to all
questions.explanation: In a democracy, franchise (voting rights) is given to all adult citizens (typically 18 years and above), regardless of property, education, or gender.

questions.question: The major advantage of secret balloting is that it
options: ensures the anonymity of the voter
extends the franchise to all adults
is faster than the other systems
questions.explanation: The major advantage of secret balloting is that it ensures the anonymity of the voter, protecting them from intimidation, coercion, or retaliation.
questions.hint: No one knows who you voted for.

questions.question: A candidate who wins an election by a simple majority, must have been voted for by
options: a good number of the voters
questions.explanation: A simple majority (first-past-the-post) means the candidate must have been voted for by a majority of the voters (more votes than any other candidate, but not necessarily more than half).

questions.question: A party system can be defined by the
options: manner the government and the parties operate
relationship between the parties and the voters
number of political parties in a country
questions.explanation: A party system is defined by the number of political parties in a country (one-party, two-party, or multi-party system) and their competitive relationships.

questions.question: One major aim of a pressure group is to
options: influence the policies of the government
install a government of its choice
implement policies that will benefit its member
questions.explanation: One major aim of a pressure group is to influence the policies of the government without seeking to capture political power themselves.

questions.question: Which of the following is the commonest means of expressing public opinion?
questions.explanation: Newspaper is the commonest means of expressing public opinion, through editorials, letters to the editor, columns, and op-eds.

questions.question: One effective way by which governments ascertain public support and reactions is through
questions.explanation: Elections are an effective way for governments to ascertain public support and reactions, as voting results indicate approval or rejection of policies and leadership.

questions.question: An acephalous pre-colonial political system is best represented by the
questions.explanation: An acephalous (headless) pre-colonial political system is best represented by the Igbo political organization, which had no central authority or single ruler.

questions.question: The administration of the capital of the emirate under the pre-colonial Hausa-Fulani political system was entrusted to the
questions.explanation: The Sarkin fada was responsible for the administration of the capital city of the emirate, managing the palace and overseeing officials in the capital.

questions.question: In the pre-colonial Yoruba society, the power of the Oyomesi were checked by the
questions.explanation: The Ogboni (secret society/council) checked the power of the Oyomesi (council of chiefs) in the Oyo Empire, serving as a balance and mediating between the Alaafin and the Oyomesi.

questions.question: The Aro system in Igboland was a
questions.explanation: The Aro system (Arochukwu) was primarily a religious organization centered around the Ibini Ukpabi oracle, which controlled trade and politics through religious influence.

questions.question: In 1879, four British companies were merged to form the United African Company by
questions.explanation: Sir George Tubman Goldie merged four British trading companies in 1879 to form the United African Company (later Royal Niger Company).

questions.question: Which of the following was a feature of the government of a British protectorate?
questions.explanation: Native Authority was a feature of British protectorates, where traditional rulers governed their people under British supervision as part of indirect rule.

questions.question: Educated elites did not like the system of indirect rule in Nigeria because it
options: did not make provision for them
questions.explanation: Educated elites disliked indirect rule because it did not make provision for them, favoring traditional rulers over the Western-educated class for administrative positions.

questions.question: The Nigerian Council was created by
questions.explanation: The Nigerian Council was created by Lord Frederick Lugard in 1914 after the amalgamation of Northern and Southern Nigeria, serving as an advisory body.

questions.question: Nationalism in Nigeria was facilitated by
options: the actions of the veterans of the two world wars and traditional rulers
the activities of Christian churches and missionaries
questions.explanation: Education and urbanization facilitated nationalism by creating a Western-educated elite who could articulate nationalist demands and organize political movements.

questions.question: The 1946 constitution was remarkable because it
options: introduced for the first time, the elective principle
questions.explanation: The Richards Constitution (1946) was remarkable because it introduced regional governments in Nigeria, dividing the country into three regions (North, East, West).

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true about the 1963 and 1979 constitutions?
options: Both had provisions for the office of the president
Both had provision for the office of a constitutional president
Both provision for the office of the Prime Minister and president
Both had provisions for the office of an executive president
questions.explanation: Both the 1963 Republican Constitution and the 1979 Constitution had provisions for the office of the president (ceremonial in 1963, executive in 1979).

questions.question: The premier of Western Region immediately after independence was
questions.explanation: Chief Ladoke Akintola was the Premier of the Western Region immediately after Nigeria's independence in 1960, having succeeded Chief Obafemi Awolowo.

questions.question: During the period 1960 to 1966, Nigeria was governed under the
questions.explanation: From 1960 to 1966, Nigeria was governed under the Westminster system (parliamentary system), with a Prime Minister as head of government and a ceremonial Governor-General/President.

questions.question: Which of the following was not established under the 1979 Nigerian constitution?
questions.explanation: The National Council on Education was not established under the 1979 Constitution; it was created by administrative action. The other councils were constitutional bodies.

questions.question: Federalism was adopted in Nigeria as a constitutional response to the problem of
questions.explanation: Federalism was adopted in Nigeria to manage ethnic pluralism (diversity of ethnic groups), accommodating regional differences and preventing domination by any single group.

questions.question: In a federal system, the power allocated to both the central and the state governments is said to be
questions.explanation: Concurrent powers are those allocated to both the central and state governments, allowing both levels to legislate on the same matters (e.g., education, health, roads).

questions.question: In the botched Third Republic, the unit of representation in the House of Representatives was the
questions.explanation: In the aborted Third Republic (Babangida transition), the senatorial district was the unit of representation in the House of Representatives, with each state divided into senatorial districts.

questions.question: Public corporations mainly differ from the ministries in that they
options: provide social services to the public
questions.explanation: Public corporations are organized as business enterprises, operating commercially and generating revenue, unlike ministries which are purely administrative and funded by government budgets.

questions.question: The privatization of public corporations is aimed at
options: making their goods and services available
allowing the public to control them
questions.explanation: Privatization aims to make public corporations more efficient by introducing private sector management, competition, and profit incentives.

questions.question: One major democratic innovation in local government administration introduced by the Babangida regime was the
options: appointment of retired military officers as sole administrators
selection of head of personnel management departments from the councillors
separation of powers between the executive and legislative arms of the councillors
questions.explanation: Babangida introduced separation of powers at the local government level, dividing functions between executive (Chairman) and legislative (Councilors) arms.

questions.question: The change in the role of traditional rulers in local government administration in Nigeria can be attributed to the
options: involvement of the military in politics
lack of support for the traditional rulers by the citizens
questions.explanation: The 1976 Local Government Reforms changed the role of traditional rulers from executive to advisory (Chief-in-Council), removing their administrative powers.

questions.question: Which of the following factors was not responsible for the military intervention in Nigeria politics in 1966?
options: willingness of politicians to relinquish power
questions.explanation: Politicians were not willing to relinquish power; in fact, their unwillingness to give up power and political crises led to the 1966 military intervention.

questions.question: One outstanding action for which the Gowon administration will be remembered in Nigeria's political history is the
questions.explanation: General Yakubu Gowon's administration is remembered for the creation of twelve states in 1967, replacing the four regions, to reduce ethnic tensions and improve governance.

questions.question: An example of Nigeria's external cultural relations is her
options: establishment of diplomatic relations with other nations
exchange of students with friendly nations
questions.explanation: Exchange of students with friendly nations is an example of external cultural relations, promoting mutual understanding and cultural diplomacy.

questions.question: At independence in 1960, the permission to establish a diplomatic mission in Nigeria was not granted to
options: the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics
questions.explanation: At independence in 1960, Nigeria did not grant permission to the USSR to establish a diplomatic mission due to Cold War fears of communist influence.

questions.question: The concept of Africa as the centre-piece of Nigerian foreign policy implies that Nigeria should
options: interfere in the internal affairs of other African States
participate in any peace-keeping operation in Africa
lay more emphasis on African issues
questions.explanation: Africa as the centre-piece means Nigeria should lay more emphasis on African issues, prioritizing African unity, development, and liberation in its foreign policy.

questions.question: The organ responsible for the general administration of ECOWAS is the
options: Authority of Heads of State and Government
questions.explanation: The Executive Secretariat (now ECOWAS Commission) is responsible for the general administration of ECOWAS, implementing decisions of the Authority and Council.

questions.question: The first international organization Nigeria joined after independence is the
questions.explanation: Nigeria joined the United Nations Organization (UNO) immediately after independence in October 1960, as it was a global body requiring immediate membership.

questions.question: The Security Council of the United Nations Organization is composed of
options: the five permanent members and other members periodically elected
eleven member states elected by the General Assembly
questions.explanation: The UN Security Council is composed of five permanent members (P5) with veto power, and ten non-permanent members elected for two-year terms by the General Assembly.

questions.question: The headquaters of the Economic Commission for Africa is located in
questions.explanation: The headquarters of the Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is located in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, the same city as the African Union headquarters.

questions.question: In a federal state, power is shared
options: between the central government and the local authorities
among the states of the federation
among the major regions of the country
between the central government and other coordinate units
questions.explanation: In a federal state, power is shared between the central (federal) government and other coordinate units (states, provinces, or regions), each with constitutionally defined powers.
questions.hint: Power sharing between centre and units.

questions.question: In a federal system of government the centre is
questions.explanation: In a federal system, the central government is superior to other components in matters of national importance (defence, currency, foreign affairs), though states have autonomy in local matters.
questions.hint: Centre has supremacy in national affairs.

questions.question: In a presidential system of government, ministers are
questions.explanation: In a presidential system, ministers are individually responsible to the president, who can appoint and dismiss them at will, not collectively responsible to the legislature.
questions.hint: Ministers answer to the president alone.

questions.question: The principle of collective responsibility implies that
options: decisions taken are defended in spite of individual opinions
those who hold different views must acquiesce
questions.explanation: Collective responsibility means cabinet decisions are defended by all ministers publicly, even if they personally disagreed during private debate, maintaining government unity.

questions.question: A government controlled by a few people for their own interests is said to be
questions.explanation: Oligarchy is government by a few people who rule for their own selfish interests, as opposed to aristocracy where the few rule for the benefit of all.

questions.question: In the parliamentary system of government, formal legislation can take the following forms except
questions.explanation: Ministerial pronouncements are not formal legislation. Formal legislation includes Acts of Parliament, Orders in Council, and Royal Proclamations, which have legal force.

questions.question: One of the distinctive features of democracy is that it
options: is not associated with one party system
questions.explanation: A distinctive feature of democracy is that it facilitates popular participation through voting, civic engagement, and representation in government.

questions.question: A hereditary system of government is
questions.explanation: A monarchy is a hereditary system of government where power is passed down through family lines, typically from parent to child.

questions.question: The separation of judicial, legislative and executive functions is designed to
questions.explanation: Separation of powers is designed to prevent tyranny (concentration of power) by ensuring no single branch becomes too powerful and abuses its authority.

questions.question: Bicameral legislatures are a common feature of
questions.explanation: Bicameral legislatures (two houses) are a common feature of federal systems, where the upper house often represents component states (e.g., Senate in Nigeria, US, Australia).

questions.question: In a federal constitutional legislative powers that are shared by two levels of government are referred to as
questions.explanation: Concurrent powers are those shared by both the federal and state governments, allowing both levels to legislate on the same matters (e.g., education, health, roads).

questions.question: The most important function of the executive organ of government is to
questions.explanation: The most important function of the executive is to enforce laws, implementing and administering legislation passed by the legislature.

questions.question: The process of learning the norms and values associated with a political system is referred to as political
questions.explanation: Political socialization is the lifelong process through which individuals learn political norms, values, attitudes, and behaviors from family, school, media, and other agents.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the civil service?
options: promoting the interest of civil servants
advising government and implementing its policies
providing information on government and its agencies
questions.explanation: The primary role of the civil service is to advise government on policy implementation and to implement government policies efficiently and impartially.

questions.question: One instrument for safeguarding the rights of citizen is
questions.explanation: Writ of habeas corpus is an instrument for safeguarding citizens' rights, protecting against unlawful detention by requiring authorities to justify imprisonment.

questions.question: Legislative control over delegated legislation can be performed through
options: withdrawal of delegated powers by the judiciary
investigation into the exercise of delegated power
approval of legislation by the Chief Justice
questions.explanation: Legislative control over delegated legislation can be performed through investigation into the exercise of delegated power by parliamentary committees.

questions.question: The feature which best differentiates pressure groups from political parties is that they do not
questions.explanation: Pressure groups do not nominate ministers or seek to form a government, unlike political parties whose ultimate goal is to control government.
questions.hint: They don't put people in office.

questions.question: The notion of equality before the law is the same as the principle of
questions.explanation: Equality before the law is a fundamental principle of the rule of law, meaning all citizens are subject to the same laws and no one is above the law.
questions.hint: No one is above the law.

questions.question: A constitution is classified as unwritten when it
options: does not emanate from the legislature
is not contained in any single document
questions.explanation: An unwritten constitution is not contained in a single document but is derived from multiple sources including statutes, conventions, and judicial decisions (e.g., UK).

questions.question: A state is said to be fascist when
options: its leader is patriotic but the citizen are not
all rights and liberties are subordinated to state interest
its citizens are fanatically in love with their leader
all rights and liberties are placed above state interests
questions.explanation: Fascism subordinates all individual rights and liberties to state interest, demanding total loyalty and obedience to the state and its leader.

questions.question: A dominant two-party system is operated in
options: the United Kingdom and the United States
questions.explanation: A dominant two-party system is operated in the United Kingdom (Conservative and Labour) and the United States (Democrats and Republicans).
questions.hint: US and UK have two major parties.

questions.question: Indirect legislation can be achieved by
options: passing of bills and making of decrees
the two legislative chambers considering a bill
questions.explanation: Indirect legislation can be achieved through judicial interpretation and precedent (case law), where courts effectively create law through their decisions.

questions.question: Naturalization is a process of acquiring citizenship by
questions.explanation: Naturalization is the process by which resident foreigners of good character become citizens of a country after meeting legal requirements (residency, language, etc.).

questions.question: The ideology which states that each person is the best judge of what is in his self-interest is
questions.explanation: Liberalism holds that each individual is the best judge of their own self-interest, emphasizing individual freedom, rights, and limited government intervention.

questions.question: The Code of Conduct Bureau was established essentially to
options: ensure the independence of the public service
enhance probity and accountability in public service
protect the right of the public
questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct Bureau was established to enhance probity (integrity) and accountability in public service, ensuring public officers declare their assets and avoid conflicts of interest.

questions.question: The British government revoked the charter of the Royal Niger Company and took over the administration of Nigeria in
questions.explanation: The British government revoked the Royal Niger Company's charter in 1900 and took over direct administration, establishing the Protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria.

questions.question: The Independence Constitution can be described as
questions.explanation: The Independence Constitution (1960) was monarchical (Queen Elizabeth II as head of state) and parliamentary (Westminster-style system with Prime Minister as head of government).
questions.hint: Queen as head of state, PM as head of government.

questions.question: Which of the following parties formed the opposition in the House of Representatives during Nigeria's First Republic?
questions.explanation: During the First Republic, the Action Group (AG) and United Middle Belt Congress (UMBC) formed the official opposition in the House of Representatives.

questions.question: The first Head of Government in independent Nigeria was
questions.explanation: Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa was the first Head of Government (Prime Minister) of independent Nigeria from 1960 to 1966.

questions.question: A feature common to the 1963, 1979 and 1989 constitutions of Nigeria was that they provided for a
questions.explanation: A common feature of the 1963, 1979, and 1989 constitutions was that they provided for a president as head of government (executive president in 1979/89, ceremonial in 1963).

questions.question: The Lagos colony and Protectorate was amalgamated with the protectorate of southern Nigeria in
questions.explanation: The Lagos Colony and Protectorate was amalgamated with the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria in 1906 to form the Colony and Protectorate of Southern Nigeria.

questions.question: During the 1957 constitutional conference, the Willink Commission was set up to
options: suggest an equitable revenue allocation formula
recommend solutions to the problem of the minorities
questions.explanation: The Willink Commission (Minorities Commission) was set up during the 1957 constitutional conference to recommend solutions to the problem of ethnic minorities fearing domination.

questions.question: The duty of an Alkali under the Hausa-Fulani political system is to
questions.explanation: The Alkali (judge) in the Hausa-Fulani political system was responsible for adjudicating disputes under Islamic law (Sharia) in the emirate courts.

questions.question: The motion for self-government in Nigeria proposed by Chief Anthony Enahoro in
questions.explanation: Chief Anthony Enahoro moved the motion for self-government in Nigeria in 1953, calling for independence in 1956, which sparked intense political debate.

questions.question: The legislative functions of the government of Benin kingdom were performed by the Oba and a council of chiefs known as
questions.explanation: In the Benin Kingdom, legislative functions were performed by the Oba and the Uzama (council of hereditary chiefs), who served as kingmakers and advisors.

questions.question: The highest ruling body under the Murtala/Obasanjo regime differed remarkably from that of the Gowon Administration because of the
options: exclusion of military governors from the council
inclusion of the Chief Justice as a member
exclusion of the Inspector General of Police from the council
questions.explanation: The Murtala/Obasanjo regime's Supreme Military Council differed from Gowon's because it included civilians as members, broadening representation.

questions.question: Decree No34 of May 1966 is also known as the
questions.explanation: Decree No. 34 of May 1966 was the Unification Decree issued by General Aguiyi-Ironsi, which abolished federalism and created a unitary state.

questions.question: In 1979, the Unity Party of Nigeria contested and won gubernatorial elections in
options: Lagos, Ogun, Imo, Oyo and Bendel
Lagos, Kwara, Oyo, Ogun and Benue
Lagos Ogun, Oyo, Ondo and Bendel
Lagos, Kwara, Ogun, Oyo and Ondo
questions.explanation: In 1979, the Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN) won gubernatorial elections in Lagos, Ogun, Oyo, Ondo, and Bendel states (the Yoruba-speaking states plus Bendel).

questions.question: Rates are usually collected in Nigeria by
options: minister of commerce in the states
questions.explanation: Rates (property taxes) are usually collected by local government councils in Nigeria as part of their internally generated revenue.

questions.question: One of the main reason for the creation of more local governments in Nigeria is to
options: make them more responsive to people's needs
weaken the powers of traditional authorities
make them more receptive to traditional rulers
establish them as the third tier in the federal structure
questions.explanation: Creating more local governments aims to bring government closer to the people, making them more responsive to local needs and grassroots participation.

questions.question: Based on its objectives, the Organization of African Unity can primarily be classified as
questions.explanation: The OAU (now AU) is primarily a political organization, focused on decolonization, conflict resolution, and political cooperation among African states.

questions.question: Nigeria's role in ECOMOG is essentially informed by her
questions.explanation: Nigeria's role in ECOMOG (ECOWAS Monitoring Group) is primarily informed by her desire for peace and stability in the West African sub-region.

questions.question: The annual budget of the OAU is approved by the
options: assembly of heads of state and government
questions.explanation: The annual budget of the OAU was approved by the Council of Ministers, which handled administrative and financial matters, while the Assembly set overall policy.

questions.question: The major strategy used by OPEC to influence oil price is by
options: influencing buyers at the international market to buy at high price
allowing member countries to produce at their discretion
increasing the supply of the commodity
questions.explanation: OPEC influences oil prices primarily by allocating production quotas to member countries, controlling the quantity of oil supplied to the global market.

questions.question: Nigeria was suspended from the Commonwealth because of her
options: complete negligence of freedom of the press
questions.explanation: Nigeria was suspended from the Commonwealth in 1995 due to the violation of fundamental human rights, particularly the execution of Ken Saro-Wiwa and other Ogoni activists.

questions.question: Nigeria's non aligned policy means that she will
options: have nothing to do with the super-powers
not take sides in international issues based on ideological considerations
avoid having any dealing with any country with ideological leanings
relate only with member countries of the Non-Aligned Movement
questions.explanation: Nigeria's non-aligned policy means she will not take sides in international issues based on ideological considerations (Cold War East-West rivalry), while maintaining relations with both blocs.
questions.hint: Not choosing sides in Cold War.

questions.question: One of the underlying principles of Nigerian foreign policy is
options: encouragement of peace keeping operations in Africa
interference in the internal activities of other countries
respect for sovereign equality of all states
questions.explanation: Respect for the sovereign equality of all states is an underlying principle of Nigerian foreign policy, recognizing that all nations are equal regardless of size or power.

questions.question: Before a new member is admitted to the United Nations Organization, its application must be approved by all
options: permanent members of the Security Council
members of the Economic and Social Council
members of the International Court of Justice
questions.explanation: Before a new member is admitted to the UN, its application must be approved by all five permanent members of the Security Council (no veto) and then by the General Assembly.

questions.question: Which of the following international organizations was in existence before the outbreak of the Second World War?
questions.explanation: The League of Nations was established in 1919 after WWI and existed before WWII (1939-1945). The UNO was founded in 1945 after WWII.

questions.question: The organ of the United Nation Organization responsible for the former colonies of defeated world war ii powers is the
questions.explanation: The Trusteeship Council was responsible for supervising the administration of trust territories (former colonies of defeated WWII powers) and helping them achieve self-government.

questions.question: The civil service cadre responsible for the general supervision and control of ministers is the
questions.explanation: The administrative cadre is responsible for the general supervision and control of ministries, serving as top-level administrators and policy advisors.

questions.question: A disadvantage of the one-party system is that it
questions.explanation: A disadvantage of the one-party system is that it negates freedom of association by preventing the formation of alternative political parties.

questions.question: Citizenship refers to the
options: social status of a person in a state
legal status of a member of a state
questions.explanation: Citizenship is the legal status of a member of a state, conferring rights, duties, and privileges under the law.

questions.question: The rule of law is a cardinal principle associated with
questions.explanation: The rule of law is a cardinal principle associated with democratic systems, where all citizens and government officials are subject to the law.

questions.question: The main function of public opinion is to
questions.explanation: The main function of public opinion is to provide direction for public policy by expressing citizens' preferences, needs, and concerns to government.

questions.question: A typical form of delegated legislation is
questions.explanation: A bye-law is a typical form of delegated legislation, where a local government or other body is authorized to make rules under a parent Act of Parliament.

questions.question: A referendum is a device to ensure that
options: legislators vote to resolve contentious issues
bye-elections are held to fill vacant positions
decisions are effected with the consent of citizens
questions.explanation: A referendum is a device to ensure that decisions are effected with the direct consent of citizens, allowing them to vote on specific policy issues.

questions.question: One main weakness of a unitary system of government is that it
options: facilitates the domination of minority groups
the constitution can be amended easily
it is run as single entity
carpet-crossing and opposition are made difficult
questions.explanation: A main weakness of a unitary system is that it facilitates the domination of minority groups by the majority, as power is concentrated at the centre.

questions.question: Pressure groups harmonize different individual concerns through interest
questions.explanation: Pressure groups harmonize different individual concerns through interest aggregation, combining similar interests into a unified position for advocacy.

questions.question: The act of removing an elected official by the electorate is referred to as
questions.explanation: Recall is the act of removing an elected official by the electorate before their term ends through a special election.

questions.question: One of the major source of a constitution is
questions.explanation: Judicial precedence (case law) is a major source of constitutional law, especially in unwritten constitutions where court decisions establish constitutional principles.

questions.question: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is
questions.explanation: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is dynamic (changing), as it evolves with events, new information, and shifting societal values.

questions.question: Coalition government arises when
options: one of the parties has a majority in parliament
no party has a majority in parliament
two or more parties co-operate to pass a bill in parliament
the ruling party is defeated in parliament
questions.explanation: A coalition government arises when no single party has a majority in parliament, forcing two or more parties to cooperate to form a government.
questions.hint: No party wins enough seats alone.

questions.question: One of the central tenets of the fascist doctrine is that the leader is
options: subordinate to the norms of the society
subordinate to the laws of the state
questions.explanation: A central tenet of fascism is that the leader (Führer or Duce) is supreme relative to the constitution, with absolute authority above the law.

questions.question: Which of the following electoral bodies in Nigeria conducted elections from 1979 to 1983?
questions.explanation: The Federal Electoral Commission (FEDECO) conducted elections in Nigeria from 1979 to 1983 during the Second Republic.

questions.question: Constitutionalism refers to the
questions.explanation: Constitutionalism refers to adherence to a constitution, ensuring that government operates within constitutional limits and the rule of law prevails.

questions.question: Under a presidential system of government, the legislature and the executive are
options: elected separately to a fixed term
elected separately to an unfixed term
appointed by the judiciary to a fixed term
appointed at the same time to an unfixed term
questions.explanation: Under a presidential system, the legislature and executive are elected separately to fixed terms, with neither able to dissolve the other.

questions.question: The Supreme Court, through its interpretation of the constitution, is a
options: participator in the politics of the state
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court, through constitutional interpretation, serves as the guardian of freedom by protecting fundamental rights and limiting government power.

questions.question: Separation of powers is a principle which enable each arm of government to
options: overlap the functions of the other
moderate the scope of the constitution
questions.explanation: Separation of powers enables each arm of government to carry out its constitutional functions independently, without interference from other branches.
questions.hint: Each branch does its own job.

questions.question: The main attributes of a state are
options: the press, the legislature, the executive and the judiciary
government, the police and the armed forces
questions.explanation: The main attributes (elements) of a state are population, territory, government, and sovereignty.

questions.question: The General Purpose Committee of the local government functions as a
options: body responsible for supervising self-help projects
committee of the local government on public relations
questions.explanation: The General Purpose Committee of the local government functions as the cabinet of the local government, making key policy decisions.

questions.question: Nigeria observed the principle of collective responsibility between
questions.explanation: Nigeria observed the principle of collective responsibility between 1960 and 1966 (First Republic), under the Westminster parliamentary system.

questions.question: The political party with the widest spread of membership in Nigeria during the Second Republic was the
questions.explanation: The National Party of Nigeria (NPN) had the widest spread of membership during the Second Republic (1979-1983), with a national presence across all states.

questions.question: Military intervention in Nigeria arose from
options: perceived incapacity of civilians to govern
civilians desire to give up power
questions.explanation: Military intervention in Nigeria arose from the perceived incapacity of civilians to govern, due to corruption, electoral malpractices, and political crises.

questions.question: Federalism was introduced in Nigeria under the
questions.explanation: Federalism was introduced in Nigeria under the Lyttelton Constitution of 1954, which established a true federal structure with division of powers.

questions.question: Regional consciousness in Nigeria was introduced by the
questions.explanation: Regional consciousness was introduced by the Richards Constitution of 1946, which divided Nigeria into three regions (North, East, West).

questions.question: How many states were created in Nigeria in 1967?
questions.explanation: In 1967, General Yakubu Gowon created 12 states in Nigeria, replacing the four regions.

questions.question: The day-to-day operation of public corporation is the direct responsibility of the
questions.explanation: The day-to-day operation of a public corporation is the direct responsibility of the management (general manager, CEO), while the board sets policy.

questions.question: Under the independence constitution of Nigeria, Dr.Nnamdi Azikiwe was
questions.explanation: Under the Independence Constitution (1960), Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe served as Governor-General (representative of the Queen), later becoming President in 1963.

questions.question: Which of the following contributed greatly to the introduction of the Clifford Constitution?
options: National Congress of British West Africa
questions.explanation: The National Congress of British West Africa (NCBWA) greatly contributed to the introduction of the Clifford Constitution (1922) by agitating for elective representation.

questions.question: In Nigeria, the Local Government Service Commission is set up by the
questions.explanation: In Nigeria, the Local Government Service Commission is set up by the state government to manage local government personnel matters.

questions.question: In the 1930s, the political movement that challenged the NNDP domination of Lagos politics was the
questions.explanation: In the 1930s, the Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) challenged the NNDP's domination of Lagos politics, eventually replacing it as the leading nationalist party.

questions.question: Residual powers in the 1979 constitution of Nigeria were vested in the
questions.explanation: Residual powers (powers not listed in the constitution) under the 1979 Constitution were vested in the state governments, meaning they could legislate on any matter not assigned to federal or concurrent lists.

questions.question: The Nigeria Public Complaints Commission as the Ombudsman suffers most from
options: poor knowledge of the workings of the public service
too many complaints to handle effectively
lack of power to enforce its decisions
questions.explanation: The Public Complaints Commission suffers most from lack of power to enforce its decisions, as it can only investigate and recommend, but not compel action.

questions.question: A characteristic of most pre-colonial government in Nigeria is that they
options: had no clear separation of powers
questions.explanation: Most pre-colonial governments in Nigeria had no clear separation of powers, with the same institutions performing legislative, executive, and judicial functions.

questions.question: Prior to the formation of the OAU in 1963, Nigeria identified with the
questions.explanation: Prior to the formation of the OAU in 1963, Nigeria identified with the Monrovia Group, the moderate faction of African states.

questions.question: In 1991, the African Economic Community Treaty was signed in
questions.explanation: The African Economic Community Treaty was signed in Abuja, Nigeria, in 1991, also known as the Abuja Treaty.

questions.question: The first African Secretary-General of the United Nations is
questions.explanation: Boutros Boutros-Ghali (Egypt) was the first African Secretary-General of the United Nations, serving from 1992 to 1996. Kofi Annan (Ghana) was the second.

questions.question: Nigerians opposed the Defence pact with Britain at independence because it
options: was forced on Nigeria by Britain
was of no benefit to Nigeria
questions.explanation: Nigerians opposed the Defence Pact with Britain because it offended their pride at independence, seeming to compromise Nigeria's newly won sovereignty.

questions.question: The Technical Aid Corps scheme in Nigeria aims at strengthening relations with
questions.explanation: The Technical Aid Corps (TAC) scheme aims at strengthening relations with African countries by providing technical assistance and personnel.

questions.question: Nigeria's quest for a leadership role in Africa hinges principally on her
questions.explanation: Nigeria's quest for leadership in Africa hinges principally on her size and population (largest population in Africa), giving her demographic weight.

questions.question: Which of the following world powers was the first to come to Nigeria's aid during the civil war?
questions.explanation: The USSR was the first world power to come to Nigeria's aid during the civil war (1967-1970), providing military equipment and aircraft.

questions.question: Nigeria's relations with African countries are underscored by its policy of
questions.explanation: Nigeria's relations with African countries are underscored by its policy of afrocentrism (Africa as the centre-piece of foreign policy).

questions.question: The foreign policy thrust of the Babangida administration was
questions.explanation: The foreign policy thrust of the Babangida administration (1985-1993) was economic diplomacy, focusing on trade, investment, and economic cooperation.

questions.question: One of the principle objectives of the OPEC is to
options: harmonize the oil policies of member countries
determine oil prices in the international market
questions.explanation: A principal objective of OPEC is to harmonize the oil policies of member countries to ensure stable oil prices and fair returns for producers.

questions.question: How many countries constitute the Economic Community of West African States?
questions.explanation: ECOWAS has 15 member states (Nigeria, Ghana, Ivory Coast, Senegal, Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Togo, Benin, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Gambia, Liberia, Sierra Leone, Cape Verde). Option says 16 but correct is 15 at founding. Note: Answer given as 16 in the key, but current membership is 15.

questions.question: The charter of the United Nations was drawn up in
questions.explanation: The United Nations Charter was drawn up and signed in San Francisco on June 26, 1945, at the United Nations Conference on International Organization.

questions.question: The two leaders that played the most prominent roles in the formation of the ECOWAS were
questions.explanation: General Yakubu Gowon of Nigeria and General Gnassingbé Eyadema of Togo played the most prominent roles in forming ECOWAS in 1975.

questions.question: The Secretary-General of the OAU is appointed by the
options: Council upon the recommendation of the Assembly
Council upon the recommendation of the Secretariat
Assembly upon the recommendation of the Council
Defence Commission upon the recommendation of the Secretariat
questions.explanation: The Secretary-General of the OAU (now AU) was appointed by the Assembly of Heads of State and Government upon the recommendation of the Council of Ministers.

questions.question: The approval of budgetary and financial matters in the United Nations is the responsibility of the
questions.explanation: The approval of budgetary and financial matters in the UN is the responsibility of the General Assembly, which reviews and approves the UN budget.

questions.question: Power differs from influence in that it is
questions.explanation: Power is coercive (involving force or compulsion), while influence is persuasive (shaping behavior through reasoning or example without direct force).

questions.question: The standing committee of legislature is one
options: that has all legislators as members
questions.explanation: A standing committee is a permanent committee with statutory responsibilities, continuing from session to session to handle specific areas like finance, defense, etc.

questions.question: Where the constitution is supreme, unconstitutional acts of the executive and the legislature can be checked by the courts through
questions.explanation: Judicial review is the power of courts to examine legislative and executive actions and declare them unconstitutional if they violate the constitution.

questions.question: The central decision-making organ of a confederation is made up of
options: politicians elected from the confederal constituencies
politicians nominated by governments of member states
questions.explanation: In a confederation, the central decision-making organ is composed of politicians nominated by the governments of member states, who retain ultimate sovereignty.

questions.question: Which of the following is true of a parliamentary system of government?
questions.explanation: A parliamentary system adheres to majority rule, where the party or coalition with majority in parliament forms the government.

questions.question: A major feature of authoritarianism is that government is
questions.explanation: A major feature of authoritarianism is that government is centralized, with power concentrated in the hands of a single leader or small group.

questions.question: The central point of capitalism, as expounded by Karl Marx, is that
options: capitalists' profit is the surplus value obtained from workers labour
workers are inherently incapable of being owners of their labour
capitalists shall always increase workers earning capacity through wages
capitalists shall always readily consent to workers' welfare demands
questions.explanation: Marx argued that capitalists' profit is surplus value extracted from workers' labor - workers are paid less than the value they create, with the difference kept as profit.

questions.question: A constitution that requires a plebiscite or a referendum to be amended is
questions.explanation: A rigid constitution requires special procedures like plebiscite or referendum for amendment, making it difficult to change.

questions.question: An important function of a constitution is that it
options: provides a framework for the study of government
facilitates cross-fertilization of ideas of governance
serves as the fountain head of authority for the exercise of power
promotes citizen participation in government and administration
questions.explanation: An important function of a constitution is that it serves as the fountain head (source) of authority for the exercise of political power, legitimizing government actions.

questions.question: When a bill passed by the legislature is vetoed by the executive, the action underscores the principle of
questions.explanation: Executive veto of a legislative bill demonstrates checks and balances, where one branch limits the power of another to prevent abuse.

questions.question: In the legislative process, a bill is a
questions.explanation: A bill is a proposal for a new law presented before the legislature for debate and consideration. It becomes an act (law) after passage.

questions.question: One of the advantages of bicameral over unicameral legislature is that it
options: takes less time for bills to be passed
prevents the passage of ill-considered bills
questions.explanation: Bicameral legislature prevents the passage of ill-considered bills by providing a second chamber that reviews, revises, and checks hasty legislation.

questions.question: The fundamental rights of citizens include rights to
options: free education, employment and freedom of thought
questions.explanation: The fundamental rights traditionally include the right to life, liberty, and property - considered natural and inalienable rights that governments must protect.

questions.question: The manipulation of boundaries of constituencies in order to win more seats is called
questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the manipulation of electoral constituency boundaries to favor a particular political party or group.
questions.hint: Drawing districts to benefit a party.

questions.question: One argument against a multi-party system is the
questions.explanation: A multi-party system is criticized for encouraging opposition and instability, often leading to coalition governments that can be weak and short-lived.

questions.question: Associational interest groups are organized to
questions.explanation: Associational interest groups are organized primarily to further and protect the specific interests of their members (e.g., trade unions, professional associations).

questions.question: Public opinion is view that is
questions.explanation: Public opinion refers to views held by the majority of the population on political or social issues, representing the collective sentiment.

questions.question: The political neutrality of civil servants implies that they
options: are not allowed to join any organization or group
are not allowed to be involved in partisan politics
questions.explanation: Political neutrality means civil servants are not allowed to be involved in partisan politics, ensuring they serve any government impartially.

questions.question: The idea of making the civil service permanent, neutral and anonymous is to
options: make civil servants a functional elite
questions.explanation: Making the civil service permanent, neutral, and anonymous is designed to enhance efficiency in administration by ensuring continuity, impartiality, and professionalism.

questions.question: The western zone of the Sokoto caliphate was administered from
questions.explanation: The western zone of the Sokoto Caliphate was administered from Gwandu, with the Emir of Gwandu overseeing the western emirates.

questions.question: Some pre-colonial Nigerian societies are described as stateless because
options: they had no formal governmental institutions
they had no definite political boundaries
questions.explanation: Stateless societies (like the Igbo) had no formal governmental institutions such as kings or central authorities, governing through kinship and village assemblies instead.

questions.question: The method used by the British to facilitate the administration of Southern Nigeria was
questions.explanation: The British used divide and rule in Southern Nigeria, exploiting ethnic and political divisions to facilitate administration and control.

questions.question: A major function of the Warrant chiefs was to
questions.explanation: Warrant chiefs were appointed by the British to take charge of local administration in Eastern Nigeria, where traditional centralized authority was absent.

questions.question: After 1945, the demand of African nationalists changed from reform to independence because
options: the second world war boosted their morale
the second world war enhanced colonial rule
questions.explanation: After WWII, African nationalists changed from demanding reform to demanding independence because the war boosted their morale, exposed colonial weaknesses, and promoted anti-colonial sentiments.

questions.question: When Nigeria achieved independence in 1960, the Head of State was the
questions.explanation: When Nigeria achieved independence in 1960, the Head of State was Queen Elizabeth II of England, represented by the Governor-General.
questions.hint: British monarch was head of state.

questions.question: The central legislature of Nigeria became bicameral in
questions.explanation: The central legislature of Nigeria became bicameral in 1951 under the Macpherson Constitution, with the House of Representatives and the Senate.

questions.question: Which of these constitution, recognized local government as a third tier of government in Nigeria?
questions.explanation: The 1979 Constitution recognized local government as the third tier of government in Nigeria, alongside federal and state governments.

questions.question: Under the 1963 constitution, item not listed in the exclusive and concurrent lists were within the exclusive competence of the
questions.explanation: Under the 1963 Constitution, items not listed in the exclusive or concurrent legislative lists fell under the exclusive competence of the regional legislatures (residual powers).

questions.question: Before Nigeria became a republic, the highest body charged with the administration of justice was the
questions.explanation: Before Nigeria became a republic in 1963, the highest body for justice administration was the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in London.

questions.question: The equivalent of a commissioner at the local government level is the
questions.explanation: The supervisory councillor at the local government level is equivalent to a commissioner at the state level, overseeing a specific portfolio or department.

questions.question: The Independent National Electoral Commission has the power to prepare and maintain the register of
questions.explanation: INEC (formerly NEC, FEDECO) has the power to prepare and maintain the register of voters, ensuring eligible citizens are listed for elections.

questions.question: The President of Nigeria is advised on the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the country by the
questions.explanation: The National Defence Council advises the President on matters relating to the sovereignty and territorial integrity of Nigeria.

questions.question: The Nigeria Youth Movement collapsed as a result of
options: shortage of funds to run its affairs
the harassments of its leadership by government
questions.explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) collapsed due to the breakup of its leadership, particularly the rivalry between Ernest Ikoli and Samuel Akinsanya.

questions.question: The first restructuring of the Nigeria Federation took place with the
options: creation of the Mid-West Region in 1963
questions.explanation: The first restructuring of the Nigerian Federation took place with the creation of the Mid-West Region in 1963, carved out of the Western Region.

questions.question: The land use decree of 1978 vested the ownership of land in Nigeria in the
questions.explanation: The Land Use Decree of 1978 vested the ownership of all land in Nigeria in the state governments, with citizens having rights of occupancy.

questions.question: The main source of financing local government in Nigeria is
questions.explanation: The main source of financing local government in Nigeria is statutory revenue allocation from the Federation Account.

questions.question: The most remarkable legacy of the 1976 Local Government Reform in Nigeria was the introduction of
questions.explanation: The most remarkable legacy of the 1976 reforms was the introduction of uniformity in structure and functions of local governments across Nigeria.

questions.question: The Murtala/Obasanjo regime in Nigeria increased the number of states from
questions.explanation: The Murtala/Obasanjo regime increased the number of states from 12 to 19 in 1976.

questions.question: A major factor that influenced the formulation of Nigeria foreign policy in the First Republic was
questions.explanation: A major factor influencing Nigeria's foreign policy in the First Republic was the colonial legacy, including ties to Britain and the Commonwealth.

questions.question: Nigeria departure from pro-west policy during the Murtala Muhammed regime was as a result of
options: the economic interests of the west in Nigeria
the growing trade between Nigeria and the East
Nigeria's concern for decolonization in Africa
questions.explanation: Nigeria departed from pro-west policy under Murtala Muhammed due to Nigeria's concern for decolonization in Africa, adopting a more radical and non-aligned stance.

questions.question: During the Civil War, the major power that expressed moral support for Biafra's self-determination was
questions.explanation: During the Nigerian Civil War (1967-1970), France expressed moral support for Biafra's self-determination, though it did not extend formal diplomatic recognition.

questions.question: The Barclays Bank and the British Petroleum Company in Nigeria were nationalized in the late 1970s for transacting business with
questions.explanation: Barclays Bank and British Petroleum were nationalized in the late 1970s for transacting business with apartheid South Africa, defying international sanctions.

questions.question: South-South cooperation is a major policy plan on which Nigeria bases her relations with
questions.explanation: South-South cooperation is a policy plan where Nigeria bases relations with developing countries (the Global South), promoting mutual cooperation and development.

questions.question: Nigeria's recognition of the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola to lead the country was made under
questions.explanation: Nigeria's recognition of the MPLA to lead Angola was made under General Murtala Muhammed in 1975, supporting the faction that eventually won the civil war.

questions.question: The first Nigeria permanent Representative to the United Nation was
questions.explanation: Chief Simeon Adebo was Nigeria's first permanent representative to the United Nations, serving from 1960 to 1962.

questions.question: In 1981, Nigeria participated in an Organization of African Unity peace-keeping force to replace Libyan force in
questions.explanation: In 1981, Nigeria participated in an OAU peace-keeping force to replace Libyan forces in Chad, which was embroiled in civil war.

questions.question: Nigeria's withdrawal from the Edinburgh Commonwealth Games in July 1986 was in protest against British
options: support to UNITA rebels in Angola
failure to impose sanctions on South Africa
questions.explanation: Nigeria withdrew from the 1986 Edinburgh Commonwealth Games to protest Britain's failure to impose sanctions on apartheid South Africa.

questions.question: The major demand of the Third world countries on the United Nations in the recent times is the
options: expansion of the permanent membership of the Security Council
post of the Secretary-General of the organization
withdrawal of the veto power from the Security council
enforcement of resolutions on the superpowers
questions.explanation: The major demand of Third World countries on the UN is the expansion of the permanent membership of the Security Council to include more developing nations (e.g., Nigeria, India, Brazil).

questions.question: Which of the following is the function of the Council of Ministers of the O.A.U.?
options: Co-ordinating the general policy of the organization
Directing the finances of the organization
Preparing the agenda of the organization's meetings
Reviewing the functions and activities of other organs of the organization
questions.explanation: The Council of Ministers of the OAU was responsible for coordinating the general policy of the organization and preparing for Assembly meetings.

questions.question: The Economic Community of West African States has made impressive progress in the area of
options: free movement of persons and right of residence
providing financial aid to its members
questions.explanation: ECOWAS has made impressive progress in the area of free movement of persons and right of residence, with citizens able to travel without visas and reside in member states.

questions.question: Delegated legislation becomes unavoidable when
options: legislators have to proceed on a recess
questions.explanation: Delegated legislation becomes unavoidable when issues under consideration are technical, requiring specialized knowledge that legislators may lack.

questions.question: One major disadvantage of public opinion is that
options: the critics of government policies are always harassed
a vocal minority claims to represent the majority
questions.explanation: A major disadvantage of public opinion is that a vocal minority can claim to represent the majority, leading to misleading impressions of popular sentiment.
questions.hint: Loud minority pretending to be majority.

questions.question: Citizenship in a modern state expresses the status of a person who possesses
questions.explanation: Citizenship expresses the status of a person who possesses full political rights, including the right to vote, be voted for, and participate in governance.

questions.question: Communism is a system which recognizes
questions.explanation: Communism recognizes the existence of the state (though it aims for its eventual withering away), with the state owning the means of production and planning the economy.

questions.question: The delineation of constituencies is a major duty of the
questions.explanation: The delineation (delimitation) of constituencies is a major duty of the electoral commission (INEC), ensuring fair representation.

questions.question: The structure of the civil service is based on
questions.explanation: The structure of the civil service is based on hierarchical organization, with clear levels of authority from top to bottom (e.g., Permanent Secretary to clerical staff).

questions.question: A common feature of a multi-party system is that government is formed by
options: the party with the highest votes
questions.explanation: In a multi-party system, no single party usually wins a majority, so government is formed by a coalition of political parties.

questions.question: To qualify for absorption into the administrative cadre of the civil service in Nigeria, an applicant must be
options: holder of a first university degree
questions.explanation: To qualify for the administrative cadre (higher-level civil service), an applicant must be a holder of a first university degree.

questions.question: The final interpretation of the provisions of a federal constitution is vested in the
questions.explanation: The final interpretation of a federal constitution is vested in the highest court of the land (e.g., Supreme Court), which serves as the final arbiter of constitutional disputes.

questions.question: A major issue that distinguishes pressure groups from political parties is
questions.explanation: The major distinction is the objective: pressure groups seek to influence policy, while political parties seek to capture political power and form government.

questions.question: Proportional representation is a system of allocating seats in the legislature based on
questions.explanation: Proportional representation allocates legislative seats based on the total votes received by each party in an election, in proportion to their share of the vote.

questions.question: The application of the principle of separation of powers seems impracticable because power is
questions.explanation: The separation of powers seems impracticable because power is fused in reality, with significant overlap between branches (e.g., executive making regulations, legislature overseeing executive).

questions.question: One major factor that differentiates the presidential from the parliamentary system is
questions.explanation: The major differentiating factor is separation of powers: the presidential system has clear separation between executive and legislature, while the parliamentary system fuses them.

questions.question: A major consequence of proportional representation is that it
options: reduces the chances of political instability
favours the development of a two-party system
questions.explanation: A major consequence of proportional representation is that it encourages the proliferation of parties, as even small parties can gain representation.

questions.question: Oligarchy is a form of government which
options: enhances the interest of the ruling few
enhances the electoral chances of the majority
disregards the views of the minority
protects the interest of the common people
questions.explanation: Oligarchy is a form of government that enhances the interest of the ruling few, who govern for their own selfish benefits rather than the common good.

questions.question: The absence of the rule of law will bring about
